title: 1948 Arab–Israeli War

infobox.value: [Kingdom of Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt") 10,000 initially; later 20,000[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") 7,500–10,000[[10]](#cite_note-nos-14)[[11]](#cite_note-15) [Kingdom of Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") 2,000 initially;[[10]](#cite_note-nos-14) later 15,000–18,000[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [First Syrian Republic](/wiki/First_Syrian_Republic "First Syrian Republic") 2,500–5,000[[10]](#cite_note-nos-14) [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") 1,000[[12]](#cite_note-Hughes-16) [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") 800–1,200 (Egyptian command) [Kingdom of Yemen](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yemen "Kingdom of Yemen") 300[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]  3,500–6,000 **Total:**  13,000 (initial) 51,100–63,500[[e]](#cite_note-17)

*Arab armies:* [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") 3,700–7,000 killed *Palestinian Arabs:*  3,000–13,000 killed (both fighters and civilians)[[14]](#cite_note-laurens-19)[[15]](#cite_note-20)

infobox.key: From top to bottom, left to right
infobox.value: * Damaged and ruined buildings and vehicles following the [Egyptian airstrike on the Tel Aviv central bus station](/wiki/Egyptian_airstrike_on_the_Old_Tel_Aviv_central_bus_station "Egyptian airstrike on the Old Tel Aviv central bus station") * Jewish soldiers raising the [Israeli flag](/wiki/Ink_Flag "Ink Flag") at the end of the war * Israeli soldier with Palestinian prisoners during the [expulsion from Ramle](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle") * Israeli forces attacking the village of [Sa'sa'](/wiki/Sa%27sa%27,_Palestine "Sa'sa', Palestine") | |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Second and final stage of the 1948 Palestine war

This article is about the international phase of the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war"). For the preceding civil war phase, see [1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine").

| 1948 Arab–Israeli War | |
| --- | --- |
| Part of the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war") and the [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict") | |
| **From top to bottom, left to right**: * Damaged and ruined buildings and vehicles following the [Egyptian airstrike on the Tel Aviv central bus station](/wiki/Egyptian_airstrike_on_the_Old_Tel_Aviv_central_bus_station "Egyptian airstrike on the Old Tel Aviv central bus station") * Jewish soldiers raising the [Israeli flag](/wiki/Ink_Flag "Ink Flag") at the end of the war * Israeli soldier with Palestinian prisoners during the [expulsion from Ramle](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "1948 Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle") * Israeli forces attacking the village of [Sa'sa'](/wiki/Sa%27sa%27,_Palestine "Sa'sa', Palestine") | |
| |  |  | | --- | --- | | Date | 15 May 1948 – 10 March 1949[[c]](#cite_note-9) (9 months, 3 weeks and 2 days) | | Location | Former [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), Sinai Peninsula, southern Lebanon | | Result | * Israeli victory * Partial [Jordanian](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") victory[[7]](#cite_note-10)[[8]](#cite_note-11) * Palestinian defeat   + Hundreds of thousands of Arabs [flee or are expelled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight") to the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/All-Palestine_Protectorate "All-Palestine Protectorate"), the [West Bank](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") and surrounding Arab states, forming the start of the [Palestinian refugee crisis](/wiki/Palestinian_refugee_problem "Palestinian refugee problem")   + Beginning of the [Palestinian Fedayeen insurgency](/wiki/Palestinian_Fedayeen_insurgency "Palestinian Fedayeen insurgency") * Arab League defeat   + Hundreds of thousands of Jews [flee or are expelled from Arab countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_the_Muslim_world "Jewish exodus from the Muslim world"), largely settling in Israel   + [Arab League boycott of Israel](/wiki/Arab_League_boycott_of_Israel "Arab League boycott of Israel") | | Territorial changes | * Establishment of the [State of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel") * [Jordanian annexation](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") of the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") * [Egyptian occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip_by_Egypt "Occupation of the Gaza Strip by Egypt") of the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") | | |

The **1948 Arab–Israeli War** (15 May 1948 – 10 March 1949), also known as the **First Arab–Israeli War**,[[f]](#cite_note-24) followed the [civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine") (29 November 1947 – 14 May 1948) as the second and final stage of the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war"). The civil war became a war of separate states with the [Israeli Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") on 14 May 1948, the [end of the British Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/End_of_the_British_Mandate_for_Palestine "End of the British Mandate for Palestine") at midnight, and the invasion by a [military coalition of Arab states](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") into the territory of [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") the following morning. The war formally ended with the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") which established the [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)").

Since the 1917 [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration") and the 1920 creation of the British Mandate of Palestine, and in the context of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and the [mass migration of European Jews to Palestine](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah"), there had been [tension and conflict](/wiki/Intercommunal_conflict_in_Mandatory_Palestine "Intercommunal conflict in Mandatory Palestine") between Arabs, Jews, and the British in Palestine. The conflict escalated into a [civil war](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine") on 30 November 1947, the day after the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") adopted the [Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine") proposing to divide the territory into an Arab state, a Jewish state, and an internationally administered [corpus separatum](/wiki/Corpus_separatum_(Jerusalem) "Corpus separatum (Jerusalem)") for the cities of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem").

The civil war began with attacks by Arab militias and mobs on Jewish areas as a reaction to the UN Partition Plan vote. As the two communities battled, the British withdrew. In April 1948, [Zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") forces launched an offensive codenamed [Plan Dalet](/wiki/Plan_Dalet "Plan Dalet"), during which they conquered and [depopulated](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_villages_depopulated_during_the_1947%E2%80%931949_Palestine_war "List of towns and villages depopulated during the 1947–1949 Palestine war") cities, villages, and territories in Mandatory Palestine in preparation for the establishment of a Jewish state. Just before the [expiration of the British Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/End_of_the_British_Mandate_for_Palestine "End of the British Mandate for Palestine"), Zionist leaders announced the [Israeli Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") on 14 May 1948.[[19]](#cite_note-25) The following morning, [Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt"), [Transjordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), [Syria](/wiki/First_Syrian_Republic "First Syrian Republic"), and expeditionary forces from [Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") launched an invasion into Palestine, taking control of the Arab areas and attacking Israeli forces and settlements.[[20]](#cite_note-26)[[21]](#cite_note-27)[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28)[[23]](#cite_note-29) The 10 months of fighting took place mostly on the territory of the British Mandate and in the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") and [southern Lebanon](/wiki/Southern_Lebanon "Southern Lebanon"), interrupted by several truce periods.[[24]](#cite_note-30)

By the end of the war, the State of Israel controlled about 78% of the former territory of Mandatory Palestine: all of the area that the UN had proposed for a Jewish state, as well as almost 60% of the area proposed for an Arab state,[[25]](#cite_note-31) including [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa"), [Lydda](/wiki/Lod "Lod") and [Ramle](/wiki/Ramle "Ramle") area, [Upper Galilee](/wiki/Upper_Galilee "Upper Galilee"), some parts of the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"), the west coast as far as [Gaza City](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), and a wide strip along the [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv")–[Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") road. Israel also took control of [West Jerusalem](/wiki/West_Jerusalem "West Jerusalem"), which was meant to be part of an international zone for Jerusalem and its environs. Transjordan took control of [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem") and what became known as the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), [annexing it the following year](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank"). The territory known today as the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") was [occupied by Egypt](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip_by_Egypt "Occupation of the Gaza Strip by Egypt").

[Expulsions of Palestinians](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight"), which had begun during the civil war, continued during the Arab-Israeli war. Hundreds of Palestinians were killed in [multiple massacres](/wiki/Killings_and_massacres_during_the_1948_Palestine_war "Killings and massacres during the 1948 Palestine war"), such as occurred in the [expulsions from Lydda and Ramle](/wiki/Palestinian_expulsion_from_Lydda_and_Ramle "Palestinian expulsion from Lydda and Ramle"). These events are known today as the [Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") for "the catastrophe") and were the beginning of the [Palestinian refugee problem](/wiki/Palestinian_refugee_problem "Palestinian refugee problem"). A similar number of Jews [fled or were expelled from the surrounding Arab states](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_the_Muslim_world "Jewish exodus from the Muslim world") in the three years following the war, 260,000 of which went to Israel.[[26]](#cite_note-32)[[17]](#cite_note-BennyMorris2-22)[[27]](#cite_note-33)

## Background

Main article: [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war")

Further information: [United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine") and [1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/UN_Palestine_Partition_Versions_1947.jpg/250px-UN_Palestine_Partition_Versions_1947.jpg)](/wiki/File:UN_Palestine_Partition_Versions_1947.jpg)

The 1947 [UN Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/UN_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "UN Partition Plan for Palestine")

Since the 1917 [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration") and the 1920 creation of the British Mandate of Palestine, and in the context of [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") and the [mass migration of European Jews to Palestine](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah"), there had been [tension and conflict](/wiki/Intercommunal_conflict_in_Mandatory_Palestine "Intercommunal conflict in Mandatory Palestine") between Arabs, Jews, and the British. British policies dissatisfied both Arabs and Jews. In 1920, the Arab leaders were very disappointed with Britain. In 1916, the British commander-in-chief in Cairo had made an agreement with the Emir of [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"): if the Arabs rebelled against the Ottoman Empire, the British would provide them with arms and money and support the formation of an independent Arab state. Around 30,000 older rifles and a smaller amount of modern weapons were supplied by the British, and a very large area from the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") to [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") was conquered.[[28]](#cite_note-34)

Britain backtracked from its promise that an independent Arab state would be formed. In 1920, Britain let French troops attack the [Arab Kingdom of Syria](/wiki/Arab_Kingdom_of_Syria "Arab Kingdom of Syria"), crushing its army and overthrowing its government. Arab opposition developed into the [1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine](/wiki/1936%E2%80%931939_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine"), while the Jewish opposition developed into the 1944–1947 [Jewish insurgency in Palestine](/wiki/Jewish_insurgency_in_Palestine "Jewish insurgency in Palestine"). On 29 November 1947, the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly") adopted a resolution recommending the adoption and implementation of a plan to partition the British Mandate of Palestine into two states, one Arab and one Jewish, and the City of Jerusalem.[[29]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Protests_in_Cairo_against_the_UN_partition_of_Palestine.jpg/250px-Protests_in_Cairo_against_the_UN_partition_of_Palestine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protests_in_Cairo_against_the_UN_partition_of_Palestine.jpg)

Protests in Cairo, Egypt against the UN Partition Plan, December 1947

The [General Assembly resolution on Partition](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine") was greeted with overwhelming joy in Jewish communities and widespread outrage in the Arab world. In Palestine, violence erupted almost immediately, feeding into a spiral of reprisals and counter-reprisals. The British refrained from intervening as tensions boiled over into a low-level conflict that quickly escalated into a [full-scale civil war](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_Civil_War_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–48 Civil War in Mandatory Palestine").[[30]](#cite_note-36)

From January onwards, operations became increasingly militarised, with the intervention of a number of [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army") regiments inside Palestine, each active in a variety of distinct sectors around the different coastal towns. They consolidated their presence in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") and [Samaria](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria").[[31]](#cite_note-37) [Abd al-Qadir al-Husayni](/wiki/Abd_al-Qadir_al-Husayni "Abd al-Qadir al-Husayni") came from Egypt with several hundred men of the [Army of the Holy War](/wiki/Army_of_the_Holy_War "Army of the Holy War"). Having recruited a few thousand volunteers, al-Husayni organised the blockade of the 100,000 Jewish residents of Jerusalem.[[32]](#cite_note-38)

To counter this, the [Yishuv](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv") authorities tried to supply the city with convoys of up to 100 armoured vehicles, but the operation became more and more impractical as the number of casualties in the relief convoys surged. By March, Al-Hussayni's tactic had paid off. Almost all of [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah")'s armoured vehicles had been destroyed, the blockade was in full operation, and hundreds of Haganah members who had tried to bring supplies into the city were killed.[[33]](#cite_note-39) The situation for those who dwelt in the Jewish settlements in the highly isolated [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") and north of Galilee was even more critical.

While the Jewish population had received strict orders requiring them to hold their ground everywhere at all costs,[[34]](#cite_note-40) the Arab population was more affected by the general conditions of insecurity to which the country was exposed. Up to 100,000 Arabs, from the urban upper and middle classes in Haifa, Jaffa and Jerusalem, or Jewish-dominated areas, evacuated abroad or to Arab centres eastwards.[[35]](#cite_note-41)

This situation caused the United States to withdraw its support for the Partition Plan, encouraging the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") to believe that the Palestinian Arabs, reinforced by the Arab Liberation Army, could put an end to the plan. However, the British decided on 7 February 1948 to support the annexation of the Arab part of Palestine by Transjordan.[[36]](#cite_note-42)

Although doubt took hold among Yishuv supporters, their apparent defeats were due more to their wait-and-see policy than to weakness.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") reorganised Haganah and made conscription obligatory. Every Jewish man and woman in the country had to receive military training. Thanks to funds raised by [Golda Meir](/wiki/Golda_Meir "Golda Meir") from sympathisers in the United States, and [Stalin's](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") decision to support the [Zionist](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") cause, the Jewish representatives of Palestine were able to sign very important armament contracts in the East. Other Haganah agents recovered stockpiles from the Second World War, which helped improve the army's equipment and logistics. [Operation Balak](/wiki/Operation_Balak "Operation Balak") allowed arms and other equipment to be transported for the first time by the end of March.[[37]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/PikiWiki_Israel_21221_The_Palmach.JPG/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_21221_The_Palmach.JPG)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_21221_The_Palmach.JPG)

A Palmach [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") tank leading a convoy

Ben-Gurion invested [Yigael Yadin](/wiki/Yigael_Yadin "Yigael Yadin") with the responsibility to come up with a plan of offence whose timing was related to the foreseeable evacuation of British forces. This strategy, called [Plan Dalet](/wiki/Plan_Dalet "Plan Dalet"), was readied by March and implemented towards the end of April.[[38]](#cite_note-44) A separate plan, [Operation Nachshon](/wiki/Operation_Nachshon "Operation Nachshon"), was devised to lift the [siege of Jerusalem](/wiki/Siege_of_Jerusalem_(1948) "Siege of Jerusalem (1948)").[[39]](#cite_note-morris2008p116-45) 1500 men from Haganah's Givati brigade and [Palmach](/wiki/Palmach "Palmach")'s Harel brigade conducted sorties to free up the route to the city between 5 and 20 April. Both sides acted offensively in defiance of the Partition Plan, which foresaw Jerusalem as a *[corpus separatum](/wiki/Corpus_separatum_(Jerusalem) "Corpus separatum (Jerusalem)")*, under neither Jewish nor Arab jurisdiction. The Arabs did not accept the Plan, while the Jews were determined to oppose the internationalisation of the city, and secure it as part of the Jewish state.[[40]](#cite_note-46) The operation was successful, and enough foodstuffs to last two months were trucked into Jerusalem for distribution to the Jewish population.[[41]](#cite_note-47) The success of the operation was assisted by the death of al-Husayni in combat.

During this time, fighters from [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun") and [Lehi](/wiki/Lehi_(group) "Lehi (group)") [massacred a substantial number of Palestinians at Deir Yassin](/wiki/Deir_Yassin_massacre "Deir Yassin massacre"). The attack was widely publicized and had a deep impact on the morale of the Palestinian population and contributed to generate the [exodus of the Arab population](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_exodus "1948 Palestinian exodus").[[42]](#cite_note-48)

At the same time, the Arab Liberation Army was roundly defeated at [Mishmar HaEmek](/wiki/Mishmar_HaEmek "Mishmar HaEmek") in its first large-scale operation,[[43]](#cite_note-49) coinciding with the loss of their [Druze](/wiki/Druze "Druze") allies through defection.[[44]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Haifa_1948_expulsion.jpg/250px-Haifa_1948_expulsion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haifa_1948_expulsion.jpg)

Palestinians being expelled from Haifa, April 1948

With the implementation of Plan Dalet, the Haganah, Palmach and Irgun forces began conquering mixed zones. The Palestinian Arab society was shaken as [Tiberias](/wiki/Tiberias "Tiberias"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"), [Safed](/wiki/Safed "Safed"), [Beisan](/wiki/Beisan "Beisan"), [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") and [Acre](/wiki/Acre_(city) "Acre (city)") were all captured and more than 250,000 [Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight").[[45]](#cite_note-51)

The British had essentially withdrawn their troops. This pushed the leaders of the neighbouring Arab states to intervene, but they were not fully prepared, and could not assemble sufficient forces to turn the tide. The majority of Palestinian Arab hopes lay with the [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") of Transjordan's monarch, King Abdullah I, but he had no intention of creating a Palestinian Arab-run state, since he hoped to annex as much of the territory of the [British Mandate for Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") as he could. He was playing a double game, being just as much in contact with the Jewish authorities as with the Arab League.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In preparation for the offensive, Haganah successfully launched Operations [Yiftah](/wiki/Operation_Yiftah "Operation Yiftah")[[46]](#cite_note-52) and Ben-'Ami[[47]](#cite_note-53) to secure the Jewish settlements of [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee"), and [Operation Kilshon](/wiki/Operation_Kilshon "Operation Kilshon"), which created a united front around Jerusalem. The inconclusive meeting between [Golda Meir](/wiki/Golda_Meir "Golda Meir") and Abdullah I, followed by the [Kfar Etzion massacre](/wiki/Kfar_Etzion_massacre "Kfar Etzion massacre") on 13 May by the Arab Legion led to predictions that the battle for Jerusalem would be merciless.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 14 May 1948, [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") declared the establishment of the [State of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel") and the [1948 Palestine war](/wiki/1948_Palestine_war "1948 Palestine war") entered its second phase with the intervention of the Arab state armies and the beginning of the 1948 Arab–Israeli War.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Armed forces

By September 1947, the [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah") had "10,489 [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"), 702 [light machine-guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun"), 2,666 [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), 186 [medium machine-guns](/wiki/Medium_machine_gun "Medium machine gun"), 672 [two-inch mortars](/wiki/Two-inch_mortar "Two-inch mortar") and 92 three-inch (76 mm) mortars".[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54)

#### Importing arms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Avia_S-199_in_June_1948_%28Israeli_Air_Force%29.png/250px-Avia_S-199_in_June_1948_%28Israeli_Air_Force%29.png)](/wiki/File:Avia_S-199_in_June_1948_(Israeli_Air_Force).png)

An Israeli [Avia S-199](/wiki/Avia_S-199 "Avia S-199"), June 1948

In 1946, Ben-Gurion decided that the Yishuv would probably have to defend itself against both the Palestinian Arabs and neighbouring Arab states and accordingly began a "massive, covert arms acquisition campaign in the West", and acquired many more during the first few months of hostilities.[[49]](#cite_note-55)

The Yishuv managed clandestinely to amass arms and military equipment abroad for transfer to Palestine once the British blockade was lifted. In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), Yishuv agents purchased three [Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress](/wiki/Boeing_B-17_Flying_Fortress "Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress") bombers, one of which bombed Cairo in July 1948, some [Curtiss C-46 Commando](/wiki/Curtiss_C-46_Commando "Curtiss C-46 Commando") transport planes, and dozens of half-tracks, which were repainted and defined as "agricultural equipment". In Western Europe, [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah") agents amassed fifty 65mm French mountain guns, twelve 120mm mortars, ten [H-35](/wiki/Hotchkiss_H35 "Hotchkiss H35") light tanks, and a large number of half-tracks. By mid-May or thereabouts the Yishuv had purchased from [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") 25 [Avia S-199](/wiki/Avia_S-199 "Avia S-199") fighters (an inferior version of the [Messerschmitt Bf 109](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Bf_109 "Messerschmitt Bf 109")), 200 heavy machine guns, 5,021 light machine guns, 24,500 rifles, and 52 million rounds of ammunition, enough to equip all units, but short of heavy arms.[[50]](#cite_note-56) The airborne arms smuggling missions from Czechoslovakia were codenamed [Operation Balak](/wiki/Operation_Balak "Operation Balak").

The airborne smuggling missions were carried out by mostly American aviators – Jews and non-Jews – led by ex-U.S. Air Transport Command flight engineer [Al Schwimmer](/wiki/Al_Schwimmer "Al Schwimmer").[[51]](#cite_note-57) Schwimmer's operation also included recruiting and training fighter pilots such as [Lou Lenart](/wiki/Lou_Lenart "Lou Lenart"), commander of the first Israeli air assault against the Arabs. Several Americans, including Schwimmer, were later prosecuted by the U.S. government for violating the [Neutrality Act of 1939](/wiki/Neutrality_Act_of_1939 "Neutrality Act of 1939").[[52]](#cite_note-58)[[53]](#cite_note-59)

#### Arms production

The Yishuv also had "a relatively advanced *arms producing* capacity", that between October 1947 and July 1948 "produced 3 million 9 mm bullets, 150,000 [Mills grenades](/wiki/Mills_bomb "Mills bomb"), 16,000 submachine guns ([Sten](/wiki/Sten "Sten") Guns) and 210 three-inch (76 mm) mortars",[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54) along with a few "[Davidka](/wiki/Davidka "Davidka")" mortars, which had been indigenously designed and produced. They were inaccurate but had a loud explosion that demoralised the enemy. Much of the munitions used by the Israelis came from the [Ayalon Institute](/wiki/Machon_Ayalon "Machon Ayalon"), a clandestine bullet factory beneath kibbutz [Ayalon](/wiki/Ajalon "Ajalon"), which produced about 2.5 million bullets for Sten guns. The munitions produced by the Ayalon Institute were said to have been the only supply that was not in shortage during the war. Locally produced explosives were also plentiful. After Israel's independence, these clandestine arms manufacturing operations were moved above ground. All of the Haganah's weapons-manufacturing was centralised and later became [Israel Military Industries](/wiki/Israel_Military_Industries "Israel Military Industries").[[54]](#cite_note-60)

#### Manpower

In November 1947, the Haganah was an underground paramilitary force that had existed as a highly organised, national force, since the Arab riots of [1920](/wiki/1920_Palestine_riots "1920 Palestine riots")–[21](/wiki/Jaffa_riots_(May_1921) "Jaffa riots (May 1921)"), and throughout the [riots of 1929](/wiki/Riots_in_Palestine_of_1929 "Riots in Palestine of 1929"), [Great Uprising of 1936–39](/wiki/1936%E2%80%931939_Arab_revolt_in_Palestine "1936–1939 Arab revolt in Palestine"),[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54) and World War II. It had a mobile force, the [HISH](/wiki/Hish_(Haganah_corps) "Hish (Haganah corps)"), which had 2,000 full-time fighters (men and women) and 10,000 reservists (all aged between 18 and 25) and an elite unit, the [Palmach](/wiki/Palmach "Palmach") composed of 2,100 fighters and 1,000 reservists. The reservists trained three or four days a month[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and went back to civilian life the rest of the time. These mobile forces could rely on a garrison force, the HIM (*Heil Mishmar*, lit. Guard Corps), composed of people aged over 25. The Yishuv's total strength was around 35,000 with 15,000 to 18,000 fighters and a garrison force of roughly 20,000.[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54)[[55]](#cite_note-61)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

There were also several thousand men and women who had served in the British Army in World War II who did not serve in any of the underground militias but would provide valuable military experience during the war.[[56]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p25-62) Walid Khalidi says the Yishuv had the additional forces of the Jewish Settlement Police, numbering some 12,000, the Gadna Youth Battalions, and the armed settlers.[[57]](#cite_note-Palestine1-63) Few of the units had been trained by December 1947.[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54) On 5 December 1947, [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") was instituted for all men and women aged between 17 and 25 and by the end of March, 21,000 had been conscripted.[[58]](#cite_note-64) On 30 March, the call-up was extended to men and single women aged between 26 and 35. Five days later, a General Mobilization order was issued for all men under 40.[[59]](#cite_note-65)

By March 1948, the Yishuv had a numerical superiority, with 35,780 mobilised and deployed fighters for the [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah"),[[60]](#cite_note-66)[[61]](#cite_note-67) 3,000 men under [Lehi](/wiki/Lehi_(militant_group) "Lehi (militant group)") and [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun"), and a few thousand armed settlers.[[62]](#cite_note-68) Irgun was eventually absorbed into the Jewish Defence Army.[[63]](#cite_note-Mi5-69) The activities of Irgun was monitored by [MI5](/wiki/MI5 "MI5"), which found that Irgun was "involved or implicated in numerous acts of terrorism" during the end years of the [British mandate in Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") such as the attacks on trains and the kidnapping of British servicemen.[[63]](#cite_note-Mi5-69)

#### Arab forces

According to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), by the end of 1947, the Palestinians already "had a healthy and demoralising respect for the Yishuv's military power" and if it came to battle, the Palestinians expected to lose. When the first violent incidents broke out in Jerusalem on the 29 November, the [Arab Higher Committee](/wiki/Arab_Higher_Committee "Arab Higher Committee"), well aware of their lack of armaments, had called for a three-day strike: the most militant Palestinian group in the city, consisting of 44 fighters, was furnished with 12 rifles, some handguns and a few kilograms of explosives.[[64]](#cite_note-70)

The effective number of Arab combatants was listed as growing to 12,000 by[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] some historians[[65]](#cite_note-71) while others calculate an eventual total Arab strength of approximately 23,500 troops,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] and with this being more of less or roughly equal to that of the Yishuv. However, as Israel mobilised most of its most able citizens during the war while the Arab troops were only a small percentage of its far greater population, the strength of the Yishuv grew steadily and dramatically during the war.[[66]](#cite_note-72)

## Political objectives

### Yishuv

[Yishuv](/wiki/Yishuv "Yishuv")'s aims evolved during the war.[[67]](#cite_note-morris_1948_397-73) Mobilisation for a total war was organised.[[68]](#cite_note-74) Initially, the aim was "simple and modest": to survive the assaults of the Palestinian Arabs and the Arab states. "The Zionist leaders deeply, genuinely, feared a Middle Eastern reenactment of the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"), which had just ended; the Arabs' public rhetoric reinforced these fears". As the war progressed, the aim of expanding the Jewish state beyond the UN partition borders appeared: first to incorporate clusters of isolated Jewish settlements and later to add more territories to the state and give it defensible borders. A third and further aim that emerged among the political and military leaders after four or five months was to "reduce the size of Israel's prospective large and hostile Arab minority, seen as a potential powerful [fifth column](/wiki/Fifth_column "Fifth column"), by belligerency and expulsion".[[67]](#cite_note-morris_1948_397-73)

According to research by Shay Hazkani, Ben-Gurion and segments of the [religious Zionist](/wiki/Religious_Zionism "Religious Zionism") leadership drew parallels between the war and the biblical wars of extermination, and states this was not a fringe position. IDF indoctrination pamphlets were distributed to recruits instructing them that God "demands a revenge of extermination without mercy to whoever tries to hurt us for no reason.".[[69]](#cite_note-75)[[70]](#cite_note-76)

[Plan Dalet](/wiki/Plan_Dalet "Plan Dalet"), or Plan D, ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): תוכנית ד', *Tokhnit dalet*) was a plan worked out by the Haganah, a Jewish paramilitary group and the forerunner of the Israel Defense Forces, in autumn 1947 to spring 1948, which was sent to Haganah units in early March 1948. The intent of Plan Dalet is subject to much controversy, with historians on the one extreme asserting that it was entirely defensive, and historians on the other extreme asserting that the plan aimed at maximum conquest and expulsion of the Palestinians. According to [Walid Khalidi](/wiki/Walid_Khalidi "Walid Khalidi") and [Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé"), its purpose was to conquer as much of [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") and to expel as many Palestinians as possible,[[57]](#cite_note-Palestine1-63)[[71]](#cite_note-77) though according to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") there was no such intent. In his book *[The Ethnic Cleansing of Palestine](/wiki/The_Ethnic_Cleansing_of_Palestine "The Ethnic Cleansing of Palestine")*, Pappé asserts that Plan Dalet was a "blueprint for ethnic cleansing" with the aim of reducing both rural and urban areas of Palestine.[[72]](#cite_note-78)

According to [Yoav Gelber](/wiki/Yoav_Gelber "Yoav Gelber"), the plan specified that in case of resistance, the population of conquered villages was to be expelled outside the borders of the Jewish state. If no resistance was met, the residents could stay put, under military rule.[[73]](#cite_note-79) According to Morris, Plan D called for occupying the areas within the UN sponsored Jewish state, several concentrations of Jewish population outside those areas (West Jerusalem and Western Galilee), and areas along the roads where the invading Arab armies were expected to attack.[[74]](#cite_note-80)

The Yishuv perceived the peril of an Arab invasion as threatening its very existence. Having no real knowledge of the Arabs' true military capabilities, the Jews took Arab propaganda literally, preparing for the worst and reacting accordingly.[[75]](#cite_note-Gelber1-81)[*[disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "Talk:1948 Arab–Israeli War")*]

### Arab League as a whole

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Clause_10_of_Cablegram_dated_15_May_1948_addressed_to_the_Secretary-General_by_the_Secretary-General_of_the_League_of_Arab_States.jpg/250px-Clause_10_of_Cablegram_dated_15_May_1948_addressed_to_the_Secretary-General_by_the_Secretary-General_of_the_League_of_Arab_States.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clause_10_of_Cablegram_dated_15_May_1948_addressed_to_the_Secretary-General_by_the_Secretary-General_of_the_League_of_Arab_States.jpg)

Clause 10 of the 15 May 1948 [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") cablegram explaining the reasons for their entry into the territory

The [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") had unanimously rejected the UN partition plan and were officially opposed to the establishment of a Jewish state alongside an Arab one.

The Arab League before partition affirmed the right to the independence of Palestine, while blocking the creation of a Palestinian government.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Towards the end of 1947, the League established a military committee commanded by the retired Iraqi general Isma'il Safwat whose mission was to analyse the chance of victory of the Palestinians against the Jews.[[76]](#cite_note-Gelber_2006_p.11-82) His conclusions were that they had no chance of victory and that an invasion of the Arab regular armies was mandatory.[[76]](#cite_note-Gelber_2006_p.11-82) The political committee nevertheless rejected these conclusions and decided to support an armed opposition to the Partition Plan excluding the participation of their regular armed forces.[[77]](#cite_note-83)

In April with the Palestinian defeat, the [refugees coming from Palestine](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_exodus "1948 Palestinian exodus") and the pressure of their public opinion, the Arab leaders decided to invade Palestine.[[76]](#cite_note-Gelber_2006_p.11-82)

The Arab League gave reasons for its invasion in [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") in the [cablegram](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Cablegram_from_the_Secretary-General_of_the_League_of_Arab_States_to_the_Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "s:Cablegram from the Secretary-General of the League of Arab States to the Secretary-General of the United Nations"):[[78]](#cite_note-84)

* the Arab states find themselves compelled to intervene in order to restore law and order and to check further bloodshed.
* the Mandate over Palestine has come to an end, leaving no legally constituted authority.
* the only solution of the Palestine problem is the establishment of a unitary Palestinian state.

British diplomat [Alec Kirkbride](/wiki/Alec_Kirkbride "Alec Kirkbride") wrote in his 1976 memoirs about a conversation with the Arab League's secretary-general [Azzam Pasha](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Hassan_Azzam "Abdul Rahman Hassan Azzam") a week before the armies marched: "...when I asked him for his estimate of the size of the Jewish forces, [he] waved his hands and said: 'It does not matter how many there are. We will sweep them into the sea.'" However, Kirkbride notes that Azzam was nervous about the impending conflict; he had not slept the night before.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

According to Gelber, the Arab countries were "drawn into the war by the collapse of the Palestinian Arabs and the Arab Liberation Army [and] the Arab governments' primary goal was preventing the Palestinian Arabs' total ruin and the flooding of their own countries by more refugees. According to their own perception, had the invasion not taken place, there was no Arab force in Palestine capable of checking the Haganah's offensive".[[75]](#cite_note-Gelber1-81)

### King Abdullah I of Transjordan

King Abdullah was the commander of the [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion"), the strongest Arab army involved in the war according to [Eugene Rogan](/wiki/Eugene_Rogan "Eugene Rogan") and [Avi Shlaim](/wiki/Avi_Shlaim "Avi Shlaim") in 2007.[[80]](#cite_note-86) (In contrast, Morris wrote in 2008 that the Egyptian army was the most powerful and threatening army.[[81]](#cite_note-87)) The Arab Legion had about 10,000 soldiers, trained and commanded by British officers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/King_Abdullah%2C_Jerusalem%2C_29_May_1948.jpg/250px-King_Abdullah%2C_Jerusalem%2C_29_May_1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Abdullah,_Jerusalem,_29_May_1948.jpg)

King Abdullah outside the [Church of the Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre"), 29 May 1948, the day after Jordanian forces took control of the Old City in the [Battle for Jerusalem](/wiki/Battle_for_Jerusalem "Battle for Jerusalem")

In 1946–47, Abdullah said that he had no intention to "resist or impede the partition of Palestine and creation of a [Jewish state](/wiki/Jewish_state "Jewish state")."[[82]](#cite_note-Sela-88) Ideally, Abdullah would have liked to annex all of Palestine, but he was prepared to compromise.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89)[[84]](#cite_note-90) He supported the partition, intending that the West Bank area of the British Mandate allocated for the Arab state be annexed to Jordan.[[85]](#cite_note-91) Abdullah held secret meetings with the Jewish Agency (at which the future [Israeli Prime Minister](/wiki/Israeli_Prime_Minister "Israeli Prime Minister") [Golda Meir](/wiki/Golda_Meir "Golda Meir") was among the delegates) that reached an agreement of Jewish non-interference with Jordanian annexation of the West Bank (although Abdullah failed in his goal of acquiring an outlet to the Mediterranean Sea through the Negev desert) and of Jordanian agreement not to attack the area of the Jewish state contained in the United Nations partition resolution (in which Jerusalem was given neither to the Arab nor the Jewish state, but was to be an internationally administered area). In order to keep their support to his plan of annexation of the Arab State, Abdullah promised to the British he would not attack the Jewish State.[[86]](#cite_note-92)

The neighbouring Arab states pressured Abdullah into joining them in an "all-Arab military invasion" against the newly created State of Israel, that he used to restore his prestige in the Arab world, which had grown suspicious of his relatively good relationship with Western and Jewish leaders.[[82]](#cite_note-Sela-88) Jordan's undertakings not to cross partition lines were not taken at face value. While repeating assurances that Jordan would only take areas allocated to a future Arab state, on the eve of war [Tawfik Abu al-Huda](/wiki/Tawfik_Abu_al-Huda "Tawfik Abu al-Huda") told the British that were other Arab armies to advance against Israel, Jordan would follow suit.[[87]](#cite_note-93) On 23 May Abdullah told the French consul in Amman that he "was determined to fight Zionism and prevent the establishment of an Israeli state on the border of his kingdom".[[88]](#cite_note-94)

Abdullah's role in this war became substantial. He saw himself as the "supreme commander of the Arab forces" and "persuaded the Arab League to appoint him" to this position.[[89]](#cite_note-95) Through his leadership, the Arabs fought the 1948 war to meet Abdullah's political goals.

### Other Arab states

King [Farouk of Egypt](/wiki/Farouk_of_Egypt "Farouk of Egypt") was anxious to prevent Abdullah from being seen as the main champion of the Arab world in Palestine, which he feared might damage his own leadership aspirations of the Arab world.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89) In addition, Farouk wished to annex all of southern Palestine to Egypt.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89) According to [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser") the Egyptian Ministry of Defence's first communique describe the Palestine operations as a merely punitive expedition against the Zionist "gangs",[[90]](#cite_note-96) using a term frequent in Haganah reports of Palestinian fighters.[[91]](#cite_note-97) According to a 2019 study, "senior British intelligence, military officers and diplomats in Cairo were deeply involved in a covert scheme to drive the King to participate in the Arab states' war coalition against Israel."[[92]](#cite_note-Zamir-2019-98) These intelligence officers acted without the approval or knowledge of the British government.[[92]](#cite_note-Zamir-2019-98)

[Nuri as-Said](/wiki/Nuri_as-Said "Nuri as-Said"), the strongman of Iraq, had ambitions for bringing the entire Fertile Crescent under Iraqi leadership.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89) Both Syria and Lebanon wished to take certain areas of northern Palestine.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89)

One result of the ambitions of the various Arab leaders was a distrust of all the Palestinian leaders who wished to set up a Palestinian state, and a mutual distrust of each other.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89) Co-operation was to be very poor during the war between the various Palestinian factions and the Arab armies.[[83]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p26-89)

### Arab Higher Committee of Amin al-Husayni

Further information: [All-Palestine Government](/wiki/All-Palestine_Government "All-Palestine Government")

Following rumours that King Abdullah was re-opening the bilateral negotiations with Israel that he had previously conducted in secret with the Jewish Agency, the Arab League, led by Egypt, decided to set up the [All-Palestine Government](/wiki/All-Palestine_Government "All-Palestine Government") in [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") on 8 September under the nominal leadership of the Mufti.[[93]](#cite_note-99) Abdullah regarded the attempt to revive al-Husayni's Holy War Army as a challenge to his authority and all armed bodies operating in the areas controlled by the Arab Legion were disbanded. [Glubb Pasha](/wiki/Glubb_Pasha "Glubb Pasha") carried out the order ruthlessly and efficiently.[[94]](#cite_note-100)[[95]](#cite_note-101)

## Initial line-up of forces

### Military assessments

Though the State of Israel faced the formidable armies of neighbouring Arab countries, due to previous battles the Palestinians themselves hardly existed as a military force by the middle of May.[[96]](#cite_note-102) British intelligence and the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") reached similar conclusions.[[97]](#cite_note-103)

The British Foreign Office and the [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") believed that the Arab states would finally win in case of war.[[98]](#cite_note-104)[[99]](#cite_note-105) [Martin Van Creveld](/wiki/Martin_Van_Creveld "Martin Van Creveld") says that in terms of manpower, the sides were fairly evenly matched.[[100]](#cite_note-106)

In May, Egyptian generals told their government that the invasion would be "a parade without any risks" and [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") would be taken "in two weeks."[[101]](#cite_note-107) Egypt, Iraq, and Syria all possessed air forces, Egypt and Syria had tanks, and all had some modern artillery.[[102]](#cite_note-Morris,_2003,_p._35-108) Initially, the [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah") had no heavy machine guns, [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery"), armoured vehicles, anti-tank or anti-aircraft weapons,[[56]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p25-62) nor military aircraft or tanks.[[48]](#cite_note-morris_2003_16-54) The four Arab armies that invaded on 15 May were far stronger than the Haganah formations they initially encountered.[[103]](#cite_note-109)

On 12 May, three days before the invasion, David Ben-Gurion was told by his chief military advisers (who over-estimated the size of the Arab armies and the numbers and efficiency of the troops who would be committed – much as the Arab generals tended to exaggerate Jewish fighters' strength) that Israel's chances of winning a war against the Arab states were only about even.[[102]](#cite_note-Morris,_2003,_p._35-108)

### Yishuv/Israeli forces

The primary Jewish military force in Palestine was the [Haganah](/wiki/Haganah "Haganah"), which was subordinate to the official leadership of the Yishuv that became the provisional Israeli government. From November 1947, with the outbreak of the [1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine"), it began reorganizing, changing from territorial militia to resemble a regular army. By April and May 1948, it was conducting brigade-sized operations.[[104]](#cite_note-110)[[105]](#cite_note-111) Mobilization of the Yishuv began during the civil war period with conscription instituted.[[106]](#cite_note-112) Following independence it was initially Israel's main military force. The Haganah was joined by two smaller independent militias, the [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun") and [Lehi](/wiki/Lehi_(militant_group) "Lehi (militant group)"). They consisted of political dissidents from the mainstream leadership and at times had come into conflict with the Haganah, but fought alongside it during the civil war and early stages of the war with the Arab states. On 26 May 1948, Prime Minister [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion") issued an order for the formation of the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") as the unified military force of Israel, which was ratified by the [Israeli cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Israel "Cabinet of Israel") on 31 May. The order called for the disbandment of all other Jewish armed forces.[[107]](#cite_note-113) Subsequently, the Haganah, Irgun, and Lehi were merged into the IDF, although the Irgun and Lehi retained independent presences in Jerusalem and the Irgun recruits into the IDF were initially placed together in their own units. After the [Altalena Affair](/wiki/Altalena_Affair "Altalena Affair"), an attempt by the Irgun to import arms in June 1948 that resulted in clashes with the IDF, the independent Irgun units within the IDF were broken up, with the soldiers dispersed between various units.[[108]](#cite_note-114) Following the assassination of [Folke Bernadotte](/wiki/Folke_Bernadotte "Folke Bernadotte") by Lehi in September 1948, the Israeli government lost its last remaining tolerance for independent militias and broke up the Irgun and Lehi remnants in Jerusalem.[[109]](#cite_note-115)

Training and experience gained by volunteers from the Yishuv in the [British Armed Forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") was decisive in building a new military. Some 30,000 Jews from Palestine served in the British military during the war. The military training and discipline, organizational skills, and combat experience they gained were of great benefit in building the IDF and fighting the war. They proved important to the Haganah's efforts to train its personnel and helped establish the IDF's General Staff, its artillery, engineering, logistics, and medical services, as well as the fledgling air force and navy. Veterans of the [Jewish Brigade](/wiki/Jewish_Brigade "Jewish Brigade"), a British Army brigade group composed primarily of Jews from the Yishuv that fought in the [Italian campaign](/wiki/Italian_campaign_(World_War_II) "Italian campaign (World War II)") towards the end of the war, were heavily represented in building the IDF, with many Jewish Brigade veterans serving as officers during the war. A handful of Yishuv volunteers served as aircrews, including pilots, and hundreds more as ground crews in the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force"), which would benefit Israel's new air force.[[110]](#cite_note-116)[[111]](#cite_note-hartoch-117) The Haganah's elite strike force, the [Palmach](/wiki/Palmach "Palmach"), had also received British training as preparation for a possible German invasion of Palestine and took part in missions against Vichy French forces in [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon").[[112]](#cite_note-118) In addition, thousands of foreign volunteers, mostly World War II veterans of Allied militaries, served in the IDF during the war in what became known as [Mahal](/wiki/Mahal_(Israel) "Mahal (Israel)"), bringing their skills and experience to the IDF. Most were Jews, but some non-Jews also served. They were both ideologically-motivated volunteers and mercenaries. Most of Israel's air and ground crews were Mahal volunteers from [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")-speaking countries, resulting in English being the main language of the Israeli Air Force during the war. Other Mahal volunteers included sailors, tank crews, doctors, and logistics and communications personnel.[[113]](#cite_note-119)[[114]](#cite_note-120)[[115]](#cite_note-Morris2008p268-121) As well as experience from World War II, the 1947–1948 civil war against Arab militias prior to the invasion of the Arab armies provided additional combat experience for units and fighters, most of whom were new recruits.[[116]](#cite_note-122)

Sources disagree about the quantity of arms at the Yishuv's disposal at the end of the Mandate. According to [Efraim Karsh](/wiki/Efraim_Karsh "Efraim Karsh") before the arrival shipments from [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") as part of [Operation Balak](/wiki/Operation_Balak "Operation Balak"), there was roughly one weapon for every three fighters, and even the Palmach could arm only two out of every three of its active members.[[56]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p25-62) According to [Larry Collins](/wiki/Larry_Collins_(writer) "Larry Collins (writer)") and [Dominique Lapierre](/wiki/Dominique_Lapierre "Dominique Lapierre"), by April 1948, the Haganah had accumulated only about 20,000 rifles and Sten guns for the 35,000 soldiers who existed on paper.[[117]](#cite_note-123) According to [Walid Khalidi](/wiki/Walid_Khalidi "Walid Khalidi"), "the arms at the disposal of these forces were plentiful".[[57]](#cite_note-Palestine1-63) According to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), on 15 May only about 60% of Haganah troops had weapons, but the amount of weapons available rapidly increased due to massive shipments from abroad as well as domestic manufacturing.[[118]](#cite_note-morris-204-124)

Yishuv forces were organized in nine brigades, and their numbers grew following Israeli independence, eventually expanding to twelve brigades. Although both sides increased their manpower over the first few months of the war, the Israeli forces grew steadily as a result of the progressive mobilisation of Israeli society and the influx of an average of 10,300 immigrants each month.[[119]](#cite_note-125) By the end of 1948, the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") had 88,033 soldiers, including 60,000 combat soldiers.[[120]](#cite_note-126)
