| [Yiftach](/wiki/Yiftach_Brigade "Yiftach Brigade") | [Yigal Allon](/wiki/Yigal_Allon "Yigal Allon") | 4,500 inc. some Golani | [Yiftah](/wiki/Operation_Yiftah "Operation Yiftah"), [Danny](/wiki/Operation_Danny "Operation Danny"), [Yoav](/wiki/Operation_Yoav "Operation Yoav"), [Battles of Latrun](/wiki/Battles_of_Latrun "Battles of Latrun") |
| [Negev](/wiki/Negev_Brigade "Negev Brigade") | Nahum Sarig | 2,400 | [Yoav](/wiki/Operation_Yoav "Operation Yoav") |

[France](/wiki/France "France") authorised [Air France](/wiki/Air_France "Air France") to transport cargo to Tel Aviv on 13 May.[[123]](#cite_note-Zamir2010p22-129) After the invasion, France allowed aircraft carrying arms from Czechoslovakia to land on French territory in transit to Israel, and permitted two arms shipments to "[Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua")", which were actually intended for Israel.[[123]](#cite_note-Zamir2010p22-129)

Czechoslovakia [supplied vast quantities of arms to Israel](/wiki/Arms_shipments_from_Czechoslovakia_to_Israel_1947%E2%80%9349 "Arms shipments from Czechoslovakia to Israel 1947–49") during the war, including thousands of [vz. 24](/wiki/Vz._24 "Vz. 24") rifles and [MG 34](/wiki/MG_34 "MG 34") and ZB 37 machine guns, and millions of rounds of ammunition. Czechoslovakia supplied fighter aircraft, including at first ten [Avia S-199](/wiki/Avia_S-199 "Avia S-199") fighter planes.

Haganah agents in Western Europe had amassed fifty 65mm French mountain guns, twelve 120mm mortars, ten [H-35](/wiki/Hotchkiss_H35 "Hotchkiss H35") light tanks, and a large number of half-tracks. The Haganah readied twelve cargo ships throughout European ports to transfer the equipment, which would set sail as soon as the British blockade lifted at the end of the Mandate.[[124]](#cite_note-130)[[125]](#cite_note-onwar-131)

In addition to arms shipments from abroad, Israel's local domestic arms industry produced substantial quantities of weapons and ammunition, as well as dozens of makeshift armored cars and trucks.[[118]](#cite_note-morris-204-124)

Following independence, the Israelis managed to build three [Sherman tanks](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") from scrap-heap material found in abandoned British ordnance depots.[[126]](#cite_note-132) Israeli agents in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") subsequently purchased 32 Sherman tanks, which arrived in three shipments from November 1948 to January 1949. Although still in serviceable condition, the tanks' gun barrels had been destroyed to prevent them from firing, so it was decided to install [Krupp field guns](/wiki/Krupp_gun "Krupp gun") that had been purchased from [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") in September 1948 with the intent to use them for field artillery, but the conversion proved complicated and it is likely that none of the Shermans with Krupp guns were ready until the war was over. However, three [75mm M3 tank guns](/wiki/75_mm_gun_M2%E2%80%93M6 "75 mm gun M2–M6") were also purchased in Italy and these were fitted onto three Shermans in time for them to take part in combat.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Tanks_of_the_Israeli_8th_Armoured_Brigade_%281948%29.jpg/250px-Tanks_of_the_Israeli_8th_Armoured_Brigade_%281948%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tanks_of_the_Israeli_8th_Armoured_Brigade_(1948).jpg)

[Sherman tanks](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") of the [Israeli 8th Armoured Brigade](/wiki/8th_Armored_Brigade_(Israel) "8th Armored Brigade (Israel)"), 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136369505171%29.jpg/250px-1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136369505171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab-Israeli_War_(997008136369505171).jpg)

An Israeli [Cromwell tank](/wiki/Cromwell_tank "Cromwell tank") to the right and a Sherman tank to the left. In addition to its fleet of Shermans, the [Israeli Armored Corps](/wiki/Armored_Corps_(Israel) "Armored Corps (Israel)") operated two Cromwell tanks.[[128]](#cite_note-Yaakov-2014-134)

The Haganah also managed to obtain stocks of British weapons due to the logistical complexity of the British withdrawal, and the corruption of a number of officials.[[129]](#cite_note-135) The equipment obtained included twelve armored cars, four of which had cannons, and three half-tracks.[[118]](#cite_note-morris-204-124) On 29 June 1948, the day before the last British troops left Haifa, two British soldiers sympathetic to the Israelis stole two [Cromwell tanks](/wiki/Cromwell_tank "Cromwell tank") from an arms depot in the Haifa port area, smashing them through the unguarded gates, and joined the IDF. These two tanks would form the basis of the [Israeli Armored Corps](/wiki/Armored_Corps_(Israel) "Armored Corps (Israel)").[[128]](#cite_note-Yaakov-2014-134)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/BrenCarrierShualeiShimshon.png/250px-BrenCarrierShualeiShimshon.png)](/wiki/File:BrenCarrierShualeiShimshon.png)

[IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") soldiers of the [Samson's Foxes](/wiki/Samson%27s_Foxes "Samson's Foxes") unit advance in a captured Egyptian [Bren Gun carrier](/wiki/Universal_Carrier "Universal Carrier").

After the first truce, by July 1948, the Israelis had established an air force, a navy, and a tank battalion.[[125]](#cite_note-onwar-131)

After the second truce, Czechoslovakia supplied [Supermarine Spitfire](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire") fighter planes, which [were smuggled](/wiki/Operation_Velvetta "Operation Velvetta") to [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") via an abandoned [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") runway in [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), with the agreement of the Yugoslav government.[[130]](#cite_note-136) The airborne arms smuggling missions from Czechoslovakia were codenamed [Operation Balak](/wiki/Operation_Balak "Operation Balak").

### Arab forces

At the invasion, in addition to the irregular Palestinian militia groups, the five Arab states that joined the war were [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") and [Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq") sending expeditionary forces of their regular armies. Additional contingents came from [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"). On the eve of war, the available number of Arab troops likely to be committed was between 23,500 and 26,500 (10,000 Egyptians, 4,500 Jordanians, 3,000 Iraqis, 3,000–6,000 Syrians, 2,000 [ALA](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army") volunteers, 1,000 Lebanese, and several hundred Saudis), in addition to the irregular Palestinians already present. These Arab forces had been trained by British and French instructors; this was particularly true of Jordan's Arab Legion under command of Lt Gen Sir [John Bagot Glubb](/wiki/John_Bagot_Glubb "John Bagot Glubb") (known as Glubb Pasha).[[131]](#cite_note-137)

Syria bought a quantity of small arms for the [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army") from [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), but the shipment never arrived due to Haganah force intervention.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

#### Arab states

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/John_Bagot_Glubb_and_soldiers_in_Ramallah_July_20_1948.webp/250px-John_Bagot_Glubb_and_soldiers_in_Ramallah_July_20_1948.webp.png)](/wiki/File:John_Bagot_Glubb_and_soldiers_in_Ramallah_July_20_1948.webp)

[John Bagot Glubb](/wiki/John_Bagot_Glubb "John Bagot Glubb") (right), commander of the Jordanian [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") with soldiers in July 1948

Jordan's [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") was considered the most effective Arab force. Armed, trained and commanded by British officers, this 8,000–12,000 strong force was organised in four infantry/mechanised regiments supported by some forty artillery pieces and seventy-five armoured cars.[[133]](#cite_note-139) Until January 1948, it was reinforced by the 3,000-strong [Transjordan Frontier Force](/wiki/Transjordan_Frontier_Force "Transjordan Frontier Force").[[134]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p27-140) As many as 48 British officers served in the Arab Legion.[[135]](#cite_note-141) The commander of the Arab Legion was a British officer, [John Bagot Glubb](/wiki/John_Bagot_Glubb "John Bagot Glubb"), also known as "Glubb Pasha". The Legion was organized into four brigades as follows:

| Military Division | Commander[[136]](#cite_note-142)[[137]](#cite_note-143) | Rank | Military Zone of operations |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First Brigade, includes: 1st and 3rd regiments | Desmond Goldie | Colonel | [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") Military Zone |
| Second Brigade, includes: Fifth and Sixth Regiments | Sam Sidney Arthur Cooke | Brigadier | Support force |
| Third Brigade, includes: Second and Fourth Regiments | Teel Ashton | Colonel | [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah") Military Zone |
| Fourth Brigade | Ahmad Sudqi al-Jundi | Colonel | Support: Ramallah, [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), and Ramla |

The Arab Legion joined the war in May 1948, but fought only in the area that King Abdullah wanted to secure for Jordan: the West Bank, including East Jerusalem.

France prevented a large sale of arms by a Swiss company to Ethiopia, brokered by the UK foreign office, which was actually destined for Egypt and Jordan, and denied a British request at the end of April to land a squadron of British aircraft on its way to Transjordan, and applied diplomatic pressure on Belgium to suspend arms sales to the Arab states.[[123]](#cite_note-Zamir2010p22-129)

The Jordanian forces were probably the best trained of all combatants. Other combatant forces lacked the ability to make strategic decisions and tactical manoeuvres,[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack2004?-144) as evidenced by positioning the fourth regiment at [Latrun](/wiki/Latrun "Latrun"), which was abandoned by ALA combatants before the arrival of the Jordanian forces and the importance of which was not fully understood by the Haganah. In the later stages of the war, Latrun proved to be of high strategic importance for its access to roads and proximity to Jerusalem, with some of the most successful Arab and Jordanian fighting occurring there.[[139]](#cite_note-145)[[140]](#cite_note-146)

In 1948, Iraq's army had 21,000 men in twelve brigades and the [Iraqi Air Force](/wiki/Iraqi_Air_Force "Iraqi Air Force") had 100 planes, mostly British. Initially the Iraqis committed around 3,000 men[[141]](#cite_note-147) to the war effort, including four infantry brigades, one armoured battalion and support personnel. These were to operate under Jordanian guidance.[[142]](#cite_note-148) The first Iraqi forces to be deployed reached Jordan in April 1948 under the command of Gen. Nur ad-Din Mahmud.[[143]](#cite_note-Pollack149-155-149)

In 1948, Egypt's army was able to put a maximum of around 40,000 men into the field, 80% of its military-age male population were unfit for military service, and its embryonic logistics system was limited in its ability to support ground forces beyond its borders.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Initially, an expeditionary force of 10,000 men was sent to Palestine under the command of Maj. Gen. [Ahmed Ali al-Mwawi](/wiki/Ahmed_Ali_al-Mwawi "Ahmed Ali al-Mwawi"). This consisted of five infantry battalions, one armoured battalion equipped with British [Light Tank Mk VI](/wiki/Light_Tank_Mk_VI "Light Tank Mk VI") and [Matilda](/wiki/Matilda_II_tank "Matilda II tank") tanks, one battalion of sixteen [25-pounder guns](/wiki/QF_25_pounder "QF 25 pounder"), a battalion of eight [6-pounder guns](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_6_pounder "Ordnance QF 6 pounder") and one medium-machine-gun battalion with supporting troops.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Egyptian Air Force](/wiki/Egyptian_Air_Force#Palestine_War "Egyptian Air Force") had over thirty [Spitfires](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire"), four [Hawker Hurricanes](/wiki/Hawker_Hurricane "Hawker Hurricane") and twenty [C47s](/wiki/C-47_Skytrain "C-47 Skytrain") modified into crude bombers.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

Syria had 12,000 soldiers at the beginning of the 1948 War, grouped into three infantry brigades and an armoured force of approximately battalion size. The [Syrian Air Force](/wiki/Syrian_Air_Force "Syrian Air Force") had forty-three planes, thirty-seven operational, of which approximately the ten newest were World War II–generation models.[[111]](#cite_note-hartoch-117)

France suspended arms sales to Syria, notwithstanding already-signed contracts.[[123]](#cite_note-Zamir2010p22-129)

Lebanon's army was the smallest of the Arab states, consisting of 3,500 soldiers, of whom only 1,000 were deployed during the war.[[134]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p27-140) In 1948, the Lebanese army contained of four infantry battalions, one artillery battalion, one armored battalion, a cavalry group as well as transport engineering and medical support units. Each infantry battalion averaged 500-550 men, with 450 soldiers per battalion, and contained three infantry companies. Only the third battalion saw battle.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHughes200526-151) According to Gelber, in June 1947, Ben-Gurion "arrived at an agreement with the Maronite religious leadership in Lebanon that cost a few thousand pounds and kept Lebanon's army out of the War of Independence and the military Arab coalition".[[146]](#cite_note-152) A token force of 436 soldiers crossed into the northern Galilee, seized two villages after a small skirmish, and withdrew.[[12]](#cite_note-Hughes-16) Israel then invaded and occupied southern Lebanon until the end of the war.[[147]](#cite_note-153)

By the time of the second truce, the Egyptians had 20,000 men in the field in thirteen battalions equipped with 135 tanks and 90 [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") pieces.[[148]](#cite_note-154)

During the first truce, the Iraqis increased their force to about 10,000.[[149]](#cite_note-155) Ultimately, the Iraqi expeditionary force numbered around 18,000 men.[[150]](#cite_note-Tripp2002p73-156)[[151]](#cite_note-157)

Saudi Arabia sent hundreds of volunteers to join the Arab forces. In February 1948, around 800 tribesmen had gathered near [Aqaba](/wiki/Aqaba "Aqaba") to invade the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"), but crossed to Egypt after Saudi rival King Abdallah denied them permission to pass through Jordanian territory.[[152]](#cite_note-158)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Saudi troops were attached to the Egyptian command throughout the war,[[153]](#cite_note-159) and estimates of their total strength ranged up to 1,200.[[154]](#cite_note-160)[[155]](#cite_note-161) By July 1948, the Saudis constituted three brigades within the Egyptian expeditionary force, and were stationed as guards between Gaza city and Rafah.[[156]](#cite_note-162)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] This area came under heavy aerial bombardment during [Operation Yoav](/wiki/Operation_Yoav "Operation Yoav") in October,[[157]](#cite_note-163)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and faced a [land assault](/wiki/Operation_Horev "Operation Horev") beginning in late December which culminated in the [Battle of Rafah](/wiki/Battle_of_Rafah_(1949) "Battle of Rafah (1949)") in early January of the new year. With the subsequent armistice of [24 February 1949](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements#With_Egypt "1949 Armistice Agreements") and evacuation of almost 4,000 Arab soldiers and civilians from Gaza, the Saudi contingent withdrew through [Arish](/wiki/Arish "Arish") and returned to Saudi Arabia.[[158]](#cite_note-164)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

During the first truce, Sudan sent six companies of regular troops to fight alongside the Egyptians.[[159]](#cite_note-165) Yemen also committed a small expeditionary force to the war effort, and contingents from Morocco joined the Arab armies as well.[[6]](#cite_note-Morris,_2008,_p._205-8)

## Course of the war

At the last moment, several Arab leaders, to avert catastrophe – secretly appealed to the British to hold on in Palestine for at least another year.[[160]](#cite_note-166)[[161]](#cite_note-Tucker528-167)

### First phase: 15 May – 11 June 1948

Further information: [1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine"), [Plan Dalet](/wiki/Plan_Dalet "Plan Dalet"), and [Israeli Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Israel_15_mai_1948.GIF/250px-Israel_15_mai_1948.GIF)](/wiki/File:Israel_15_mai_1948.GIF)

Areas under Israeli control in blue on 15 May 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg/250px-1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab_Israeli_War_-_May_15-June_10.svg)

Arab offensive, 15 May – 10 June 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Butterfly_Armored-car_Gvar-Am-israel1948.jpg/250px-Butterfly_Armored-car_Gvar-Am-israel1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butterfly_Armored-car_Gvar-Am-israel1948.jpg)

A "Butterfly" improvised armored car of the Haganah at [Kibbutz](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") [Dorot](/wiki/Dorot "Dorot") in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"), April 1948. The armored car is based on [CMP](/wiki/Canadian_Military_Pattern_truck "Canadian Military Pattern truck")-15 truck. The car has brought supplies to the kibbutz. Children of kibbutzim in the Negev were later evacuated by these cars ahead of the Egyptian advance.

The [civil war in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_civil_war_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 civil war in Mandatory Palestine") became a war between separate [states](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state") with the [declaration of the establishment](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") of the [State of Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") on 14 May 1948, a few hours before the termination of the [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") at midnight. The following morning, the [regular armies](/wiki/Regular_army "Regular army") of neighbouring Arab states – [Egypt](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Egypt "Kingdom of Egypt"), [Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan") and [Syria](/wiki/Syrian_Republic_(1946%E2%80%9363) "Syrian Republic (1946–63)") – invaded the region.[[162]](#cite_note-168)[[163]](#cite_note-169)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Through [Plan Dalet](/wiki/Plan_Dalet "Plan Dalet"), Zionist forces had already, from 1 April down to 14 May, conducted 8 of their 13 full-scale military operations outside of the area allotted to a Jewish state by partition, and the operational commander [Yigal Allon](/wiki/Yigal_Allon "Yigal Allon") later stated that had it not been for the Arab invasion, Haganah forces would have reached 'the natural borders of western Israel.'[[164]](#cite_note-170) By 15 May 1948, when the Mandate formally expired and the State of Israel came into being, Israel controlled two contiguous and connected strips of Palestine which contained the main Jewish settlement concentrations. One ran along the [coastal plain](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain") from [Rosh Hanikra](/wiki/Rosh_HaNikra_(kibbutz) "Rosh HaNikra (kibbutz)") to [Rehovot](/wiki/Rehovot "Rehovot"), with two additional appendages emanating from its southern end, one running to [West Jerusalem](/wiki/West_Jerusalem "West Jerusalem") and the other to the Jewish settlement bloc in the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") which was connected by a sliver of land near [Negba](/wiki/Negba "Negba"). The other strip was in the [Galilee Panhandle](/wiki/Galilee_Panhandle "Galilee Panhandle"), [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley "Jordan Valley"), and [Beit She'an Valley](/wiki/Beit_She%27an_Valley "Beit She'an Valley"). These two strips were narrowly linked by the Israeli-held [Jezreel Valley](/wiki/Jezreel_Valley "Jezreel Valley"). The Arabs held the rest of Palestine, including a small [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army")-supported enclave just south of [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa").[[165]](#cite_note-171)

Although the Arab invasion was denounced by the United States, the Soviet Union, and UN secretary-general [Trygve Lie](/wiki/Trygve_Lie "Trygve Lie"), it found support from the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") and other UN member states.[[161]](#cite_note-Tucker528-167)

The initial Arab plans called for Syrian and Lebanese forces to invade from north while Jordanian and Iraqi forces were to invade from east in order to meet at Nazareth and then to push forward together to Haifa. In the south, the Egyptians were to advance and take Tel Aviv.[[166]](#cite_note-Gelber2006p130-172) At the Arab League meeting in Damascus on 11–13 May, Abdullah rejected the plan, which served Syrian interests, using the fact his allies were afraid to go to war without his army. He proposed that the Iraqis attack the Jezreel valley and the Arab Legion enter Ramallah and Nablus and link with the Egyptian army at Hebron,[[166]](#cite_note-Gelber2006p130-172) which was more in compliance with his political objective to occupy the territory allocated to the Arab State by the partition plan and promises not to invade the territory allocated to the Jewish State by the partition plan. In addition, Lebanon decided not to take part in the war at the last minute, due to the still-influential Christians' opposition, encouraged by Jewish bribes.[[167]](#cite_note-173)

Intelligence provided by the [French consulate in Jerusalem](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_France_in_Jerusalem "Consulate General of France in Jerusalem") on 12 May 1948 on the Arab armies' invading forces and their revised plan to invade the new state contributed to Israel's success in withstanding the Arab invasion.[[168]](#cite_note-Zamir2010p21-174)

The first mission of the Jewish forces was to hold on against the Arab armies and stop them, although the Arabs began the war with major advantages such as the initiative and vastly superior firepower.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

#### Southern front – Negev

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Nirim1948_1.jpg/250px-Nirim1948_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nirim1948_1.jpg)

Israeli soldiers in [Nirim](/wiki/Nirim "Nirim")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Negba1948Defenses.jpg/250px-Negba1948Defenses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Negba1948Defenses.jpg)

Israeli soldiers in [Negba](/wiki/Negba "Negba")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Nitzanim_bombarded.jpg/250px-Nitzanim_bombarded.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nitzanim_bombarded.jpg)

[Nitzanim](/wiki/Nitzanim "Nitzanim") after the Egyptian bombardment

The Egyptian force, the largest among the Arab armies, invaded from the south. The Egyptian invasion was preceded by the entry of smaller forces of [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood") volunteers in April and Egyptian Army volunteers on 6 May, before the end of the Mandate.[[170]](#cite_note-morris-233-176)

With the end of the Mandate, the Egyptian task force, composed of Egyptian troops and Muslim Brotherhood volunteers, entered the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"). The main Egyptian force advanced north in the direction of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") while a mixed force of Egyptian troops and Muslim Brotherhood volunteers broke off from the main force and advanced east, occupying [Beersheba](/wiki/Beersheba "Beersheba") on 19 May before advancing to the [Hebron Hills](/wiki/Hebron_Hills "Hebron Hills"). It participated in combat alongside the Arab Legion and local irregulars at kibbutz [Ramat Rachel](/wiki/Ramat_Rachel "Ramat Rachel"). The presence of Israeli [kibbutzim](/wiki/Kibbutz "Kibbutz") along the path of main force's thrust north was seen as a threat due to their potential to cut off forward units and harass supply convoys. To secure their flanks, the Egyptians attacked and laid siege to numerous kibbutzim, expending great resources in attempting to capture them.[[171]](#cite_note-morris-235-177)[[172]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p56-178)[[173]](#cite_note-179)[[174]](#cite_note-180) The defenders of these settlements held out fiercely for days against vastly superior forces, and managed to buy valuable time for the Israelis to reposition their forces and deploy the heavy weaponry now entering the country.[[171]](#cite_note-morris-235-177)

On 15 May 1948, the Egyptians attacked two settlements: [Nirim](/wiki/Battle_of_Nirim "Battle of Nirim"), using artillery, armored cars carrying cannons, and [Bren carriers](/wiki/Bren_carrier "Bren carrier");[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28) and [Kfar Darom](/wiki/Battles_of_Kfar_Darom "Battles of Kfar Darom")[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28) using artillery, tanks and aircraft. The Egyptians' attacks met fierce resistance from the few and lightly armed defenders of both settlements, and failed. On 19 May the Egyptians attacked [Yad Mordechai](/wiki/Battle_of_Yad_Mordechai "Battle of Yad Mordechai"), where an inferior force of 100 Israelis armed with nothing more than rifles, a medium machine gun and a [PIAT](/wiki/PIAT "PIAT") anti-tank weapon, held up a column of 2,500 Egyptians, well-supported by armor, artillery and aircraft, for five days.[[175]](#cite_note-181) The Egyptians took heavy losses, while the losses sustained by the defenders were comparatively light.[[172]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p56-178) A number of other kibbutzim were heavily shelled but not assaulted.[[176]](#cite_note-182)

During the last week of May, Israeli units of the [Negev](/wiki/Negev_Brigade "Negev Brigade") and [Givati Brigades](/wiki/Givati_Brigade "Givati Brigade") harassed the Egyptians. In addition, the embryonic [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") periodically bombed the [Gaza City](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City") area, where the Egyptian force's headquarters was located, using converted civilian aircraft. On 24 May, the Egyptians reached [Majdal](/wiki/Ashkelon "Ashkelon") and made it their headquarters, briefly stopping and setting up a defensive perimeter.[[177]](#cite_note-183)

The Egyptians achieved a crucial success when a battalion advanced east from Majdal and managed to link up with the Egyptian force in the Hebron Hills. The Egyptians dug themselves in, cutting off numerous Israeli settlements as well as the [Negev Brigade](/wiki/Negev_Brigade "Negev Brigade"). However, this also resulted in Egyptian forces becoming more overstretched.[[178]](#cite_note-morris-240-184)

On 28 May the Egyptians renewed their northern advance, and stopped at a destroyed bridge north to [Isdud](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod"). The Givati Brigade reported this advance but no fighters were sent to confront the Egyptians. Had the Egyptians wished to continue their advance northward towards Tel Aviv, the only Israeli forces positioned to block them were two and a half companies of the Givati Brigade.[[179]](#cite_note-185)[[178]](#cite_note-morris-240-184)[[180]](#cite_note-186)

On 2 June, an Egyptian battalion [attacked Negba](/wiki/Battles_of_Negba#First_skirmishes_and_battle_of_June_2 "Battles of Negba") and was beaten back by the kibbutz's 140 defenders. The Israelis lost 8 killed and 11 wounded while inflicting an estimated 100 casualties on the Egyptians.[[181]](#cite_note-187)

From 29 May to 3 June, Israeli forces stopped the Egyptian drive north in [Operation Pleshet](/wiki/Operation_Pleshet "Operation Pleshet"). It began with attacks by Israel's fledgling air force. Isrseli aircraft attacked Egyptian positions at Isdud. The Israeli planes dropped 70 kilogram bombs and strafed enemy positions, although their machine guns quickly jammed. One plane was shot down and another crashed. The attacks caused the Egyptians to scatter, and they had lost the initiative by the time they had regrouped. Following the air attacks, the [Givati Brigade](/wiki/Givati_Brigade "Givati Brigade") launched a counterattack against Egyptian forces in Isdud supported by a battery of 65mm [*Napoleonchik*](/wiki/Canon_de_65_M_(montagne)_modele_1906 "Canon de 65 M (montagne) modele 1906") cannons and two 120mm mortars. The counterattack was repulsed, although Israeli troops managed to briefly capture houses on the village's outskirts before being pushed back. Despite having held their ground, the Egyptian command was alarmed by the counterattack. They feared that their forces might be cut off. This fear was exacerbated when Givati Brigade troops ambushed an Egyptian supply column just south of Isdud. The Egyptian offensive was halted as Egypt changed its strategy from offensive to defensive, and the initiative shifted to Israel.[[178]](#cite_note-morris-240-184) The Israelis lost 45 killed or missing, 50 wounded, and 5 captured.[[182]](#cite_note-188) Egyptian losses were variously reported as 7-15 killed and 18-30 wounded, although these may only be partial figures.[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)

On 6 June, in the [Battle of Nitzanim](/wiki/Battle_of_Nitzanim "Battle of Nitzanim"), Egyptian forces attacked the kibbutz of [Nitzanim](/wiki/Nitzanim "Nitzanim"), located between Majdal and Isdud, and the Israeli defenders surrendered after resisting for five days. Shortly before the first truce was to come into effect, an Israeli counterattack to retake the kibbutz failed and the Israelis retreated to nearby Hill 69, which was subsequently attacked and conquered by the Egyptians causing the Israelis to retreat further. The Egyptians then captured a major crossroads and attempted to continue towards [Beit Daras](/wiki/Beit_Daras "Beit Daras") and [Be'er Tuvia](/wiki/Be%27er_Tuvia "Be'er Tuvia") but ran into fierce resistance and withdrew. However, Israeli forces managed to occupy a number of hilltop positions and villages along the front line, although they failed to conquer the [Tegart fort](/wiki/Tegart_fort "Tegart fort") at [Iraq Suwaydan](/wiki/Iraq_Suwaydan "Iraq Suwaydan").[[185]](#cite_note-191)[[186]](#cite_note-morris-260-192) By the time the first truce came into effect, the Egyptians were in scattered positions across the Negev to the Hebron area and incapable of mounting a serious offensive.

#### Jerusalem and Latrun

Further information: [Battle for Jerusalem](/wiki/Battle_for_Jerusalem "Battle for Jerusalem") and [Battles of Latrun (1948)](/wiki/Battles_of_Latrun_(1948) "Battles of Latrun (1948)")

The heaviest fighting occurred in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Battle_for_Jerusalem_(1948) "Battle for Jerusalem (1948)") and on the Jerusalem – Tel Aviv road, between Jordan's [Arab Legion](/wiki/Arab_Legion "Arab Legion") and Israeli forces.[[187]](#cite_note-193)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Porat_Yosef_attack.jpg/250px-Porat_Yosef_attack.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porat_Yosef_attack.jpg)

Arab Legion soldiers during fighting in the Jewish Quarter

With the termination of the Mandate, the Arab Legion entered eastern Palestine. Its units swiftly reached [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho"), [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), and [Latrun](/wiki/Latrun "Latrun"), facing no resistance. The original Jordanian plan had been to avoid [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), as Jordan had promised the British a peaceful takeover of Arab areas in eastern Palestine and entering Jerusalem would both go against the UN plan for it to be an international zone and result in combat. However, after British control over Jerusalem ended, the Haganah and [Irgun](/wiki/Irgun "Irgun") rapidly seized control in parts of the city, taking over British outposts in the Old City in [Operation Shfifon](/wiki/Operation_Shfifon "Operation Shfifon") a day before the Israeli declaration of independence followed by the rapid seizure of numerous other areas of the city in [Operation Pitchfork](/wiki/Operation_Pitchfork "Operation Pitchfork"). Arab refugees fled Jerusalem in large numbers, and the city's Arab notables sent appeals to [King Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan") and the Arab Legion's commander [John Bagot Glubb](/wiki/John_Bagot_Glubb "John Bagot Glubb") asking for help. In addition, King Abdullah was probably also motivated to intervene over the city's political and religious signifiance, as well as the fact that he would be blamed for the fall of [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem") by the Arab world, Israeli forces could potentially advance to Jericho from Jerusalem and cut off his forces, the graves of his father and brother Faisal were located there, and annexing an area as important as that would make his kingdom more significant. The easy occupation of eastern Palestine also likely gave him an appetite for further conquest as he talked of conquering [West Jerusalem](/wiki/West_Jerusalem "West Jerusalem") and Tel Aviv for several days. It was finally decided to assault Jerusalem.[[188]](#cite_note-morris-211-194) King Abdullah ordered Glubb to enter Jerusalem on 17 May.[[189]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p62-195)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Arab_Legion_Platoon_on_the_walls_of_Old_Jerusalem_%281948%29.jpg/250px-Arab_Legion_Platoon_on_the_walls_of_Old_Jerusalem_%281948%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arab_Legion_Platoon_on_the_walls_of_Old_Jerusalem_(1948).jpg)

An Arab Legion platoon on the Old City walls

Combat in Jerusalem had begun on 16 May when Arab irregulars attacked the [Jewish Quarter](/wiki/Jewish_Quarter_(Jerusalem) "Jewish Quarter (Jerusalem)") of the [Old City](/wiki/Old_City_of_Jerusalem "Old City of Jerusalem"). Fighting continued into the next day, with the Jewish Quarter's Haganah defenders holding out despite heavy losses, a shortage of ammunition, and the calls of the quarter's rabbis to surrender for fear of a massacre. On 17-18 May, the Haganah launched an assault to break into the Old City which was stopped at the [Jaffa Gate](/wiki/Jaffa_Gate "Jaffa Gate"), while another attack by the [Harel Brigade](/wiki/Harel_Brigade "Harel Brigade") at the southern wall resulted in the capture of [Mount Zion](/wiki/Mount_Zion "Mount Zion"). The Haganah followed up on this with an assault on [Zion Gate](/wiki/Zion_Gate "Zion Gate"), which was captured by a [Palmach](/wiki/Palmach "Palmach") unit. Haganah porters then delivered ammunition and extra weapons to the Jewish Quarter, but the Palmach force, too small to comfortably hold the Zion Gate and alley to the Old City, subsequently retreated.[[190]](#cite_note-morris-217-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Operation_Yoram.jpg/250px-Operation_Yoram.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Yoram.jpg)

Bombardment of Latrun during Operation Yoram

On 19 May, Arab Legion forces advanced to Jerusalem from Ramallah and entered the city. Irgun fighters resisted their advance at [Sheikh Jarrah](/wiki/Sheikh_Jarrah "Sheikh Jarrah") and the Police School and were defeated, losing 6 dead and 15 wounded. The Arab Legion advanced to the [Damascus Gate](/wiki/Damascus_Gate "Damascus Gate"). However, their forces along the Ramallah-Jerusalem road were still enfiladed by Israeli positions. The Legion's main attack at [Mandelbaum Gate](/wiki/Mandelbaum_Gate "Mandelbaum Gate") was beaten back with three armored cars destroyed, and its assaults on [Mount Scopus](/wiki/Mount_Scopus "Mount Scopus"), [Sanhedria](/wiki/Sanhedria "Sanhedria"), and [Beit Yisrael](/wiki/Beit_Yisrael "Beit Yisrael") were also repulsed. On 20 May, a Legion armored push against Haganah positions at the Notre Dame monastery, located at the northern wall of the Old City, was repelled with the loss of several armored cars.[[188]](#cite_note-morris-211-194) On 22 May, Arab Legion forces again assaulted the Notre Dame monastery. The Legion convoy took fire from Haganah positions as it advanced through the city towards the monastery, which was answered with small arms, mortar, and artillery fire. The attack on the monastery was poorly executed and repulsed with one armored car disabled. On the following day, a better-organized attack was mounted with infantry, armored cars, mortars, and artillery. However, the attack again faltered in the face of fierce resistance, with two armored cars knocked out. One Legion company managed to enter the ground floor of the monastery and engaged in close-quarters fighting but the rest of the force was pinned down by Haganah fire and could not join the company inside, which was ultimately forced to retreat. Combat around the monastery continued for hours before the Arab Legion retreated. The Legion had taken dozens of casualties.[[191]](#cite_note-197)

At the southern edge of Jerusalem, kibbutz [Ramat Rachel](/wiki/Ramat_Rachel "Ramat Rachel") was attacked on 19 May by a mixed force composed of Arab Legion units, local Arab irregulars, and the force of Egyptian soldiers and Muslim Brotherhood volunteers that had advanced from the Negev after splitting off from the main Egyptian force. After three days of bombardment which almost leveled the kibbutz, an infantry assault on 22 May captured it and the defenders retreated. Haganah forces returned and the kibbutz repeatedly changed hands before the final battle began on 24 May, when Haganah and Irgun forces fiercely held out for two days before counterattacking, driving the Arab forces out and conquering the nearby [Mar Elias Monastery](/wiki/Mar_Elias_Monastery "Mar Elias Monastery"). The Arab force lost over 100 killed in the fighting while the Israelis lost 26 killed and 84 wounded. The Israeli victory at Ramat Rachel secured the southern entrance to the city.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

* [![Arabs plundering the Jewish Quarter after the expulsion of its inhabitants](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Looting_the_Jewish_Quarter.jpg/250px-Looting_the_Jewish_Quarter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Looting_the_Jewish_Quarter.jpg "Arabs plundering the Jewish Quarter after the expulsion of its inhabitants")

  Arabs plundering the Jewish Quarter after the expulsion of its inhabitants

The Arab Legion also occupied the Zion Gate on 19 May following the Haganah retreat from the area, cutting off the Jewish Quarter again, and the assault on the quarter resumed, this time by the Legion together with Arab irregulars, about half of whom were from the [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army"). The attack was supported by artillery, mortars, and armored cars, and Legion soldiers methodically blew up every building they took. The defenders were outnumbered and outgunned. The Harel Brigade launched poorly planned and undermanned attacks to try to break into the Jewish Quarter which were beaten back. Jewish morale plummeted after the [Hurva Synagogue](/wiki/Hurva_Synagogue "Hurva Synagogue") was captured and blown up. On 28 May, the Jewish Quarter surrendered. Of the defenders, 39 had been killed and 134 wounded. The inhabitants were expelled, with 1,200 being escorted to Israeli-held West Jerusalem alongside some seriously wounded defenders, while 290 males, two-thirds of whom were civilians, were taken prisoner. The Jews had to be escorted out by the Arab Legion to protect them against Palestinian Arab mobs that intended to massacre them. Legion soldiers killed at least two Arab civilians while guarding the Jews. The Jewish Quarter was subsequently pillaged and razed by an Arab mob.[[194]](#cite_note-morris-219-200) According to the official Jordanian military account, the Arab Legion lost 14 killed and 25 wounded in fighting for the Old City.[[195]](#cite_note-201)

Simultaneously, the Arab Legion moved to cut off the Jerusalem-Tel Aviv road. Glubb felt that in order to hold onto East Jerusalem he had to prevent the Israelis, who had many more troops, from reinforcing their units in Jerusalem. The strategic hilltop of [Latrun](/wiki/Latrun "Latrun"), which had a [Tegart fort](/wiki/Tegart_fort "Tegart fort"), was deemed the ideal location. Arab Legion forces firmly occupied Latrun on 18 May and held the area together with local Arab irregulars. From the high ground they were able to shell Israeli traffic along the road to Tel Aviv. As a result, Israeli-held [West Jerusalem](/wiki/West_Jerusalem "West Jerusalem") was effectively placed under siege. Supplies to Israeli fighters and civilians in Jerusalem were cut off.[[189]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p62-195)[[196]](#cite_note-202)[[194]](#cite_note-morris-219-200) Though some supplies, mostly munitions, were airdropped into the city, the shortage of food, water, fuel, medicine, and ammunition in West Jerusalem was acute.[[189]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p62-195)

  Building the Burma Road

* [![Area map](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Latroun_%2811_juin%29.png/250px-Latroun_%2811_juin%29.png)](/wiki/File:Latroun_(11_juin).png "Area map")

  Area map

The Israelis attempted to take the Latrun fortress in a series of battles lasting from 24 May to 18 July. The Arab Legion held Latrun and managed to repulse the attacks.[[189]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p62-195) The first attack, dubbed Operation Bin-Nun Alef, was a disastrous failure. The attack was decisively repelled with the Israelis losing 72 killed, 140 wounded, and 6 captured while the Arab Legion and Arab irregulars lost 5 killed and 6 wounded.[[197]](#cite_note-203) They tried again with Operation Bin-Nun Bet on 30-31 May. This attack met more success, with the Israelis managing to capture the monastery and half the village on the hill and reaching the perimeter of the fort, with some soldiers even breaching into the fort before being killed inside, but in the end the Israelis retreated. The attack cost the Israelis 44 killed while the Arab Legion lost between 12 and 20 killed, with the fort's commander among the dead.[[198]](#cite_note-morris-225-204)[[199]](#cite_note-205)[[200]](#cite_note-206) Israeli forces made one more attempt to capture Latrun before the first truce with Operation Yoram, an attack by two battalions on the night of 8-9 June. The attack made some progress and managed to cause a partial retreat of the Arab Legion but due to confusion, approaching daylight, and reports of heavy casualties, the attackers were ordered to retreat. One of the Israeli battalions lost 16 killed and 79 wounded while the other had a handful of casualties. The Arab Legion suffered several dozen casualties.[[198]](#cite_note-morris-225-204)

On 10 June, a day before the first truce came into effect, a battalion-sized force of Arab Legion soldiers and Arab irregulars equipped with armored cars attacked kibbutz [Gezer](/wiki/Gezer_(kibbutz) "Gezer (kibbutz)") and captured it after a battle in which 29 of the kibbutz's defenders and 2 Legion soldiers were killed. IDF forces in the area failed to reinforce the kibbutz due to poor communications. After the Legion withdrew the kibbutz was reoccupied by Israeli forces in the evening.[[198]](#cite_note-morris-225-204)

The besieged Israeli-held West Jerusalem was only saved via the opening of the so-called "[Burma Road](/wiki/Burma_Road_(Israel) "Burma Road (Israel)")", a makeshift bypass road built by Israeli forces that allowed Israeli supply convoys to pass into Jerusalem.[[189]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p62-195) [Palmach](/wiki/Palmach "Palmach") scouts had noticed the potential for an alternate route bypassing Latrun. After the Haganah took [Bayt Jiz](/wiki/Bayt_Jiz "Bayt Jiz") and [Bayt Susin](/wiki/Bayt_Susin "Bayt Susin") on 28 May it had a continuous route from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem, and engineering works to build a road subsequently began. The Arab Legion attempted to disrupt the works with artillery fire and harassment by patrols, but was unsuccessful. A new water pipeline was also laid to replace the one blocked by the Legion at Latrun. On 10 June, the road opened and transport trucks were able to access West Jerusalem, bringing in supplies.[[201]](#cite_note-207)

#### Northern Samaria

Further information: [Battle of Gesher](/wiki/Battle_of_Gesher "Battle of Gesher") and [Battle of Jenin (1948)](/wiki/Battle_of_Jenin_(1948) "Battle of Jenin (1948)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Afulahagana.jpg/250px-Afulahagana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afulahagana.jpg)

Israeli soldiers in [Afula](/wiki/Afula "Afula")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136230705171%29.jpg/250px-1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136230705171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab-Israeli_War_(997008136230705171).jpg)

Israeli soldiers in the [Mount Gilboa](/wiki/Mount_Gilboa "Mount Gilboa") area

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Iraqi_army_in_jeneen_palestine_1948.jpg/250px-Iraqi_army_in_jeneen_palestine_1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iraqi_army_in_jeneen_palestine_1948.jpg)

Iraqi soldiers and an armored car near Jenin

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/1948_Arab-Israeli_War_997008136998005171.jpg/250px-1948_Arab-Israeli_War_997008136998005171.jpg)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab-Israeli_War_997008136998005171.jpg)

Israeli soldiers at Qaqun

An Iraqi brigade group consisting of two infantry battalions, one armored car battalion, and one artillery battalion was sent to [Mafraq](/wiki/Mafraq "Mafraq") in northern Transjordan in late April to prepare for action in Palestine, and crossed the Jordan River on 15 May. The Iraqis attacked kibbutz [Gesher](/wiki/Gesher,_Israel "Gesher, Israel") beginning with an artillery barrage. On 16 May, the Iraqis captured nearby Camel Hill and launched assaults on the kibbutz and a nearby police fort. The defenders, who received air support from [Piper Cubs](/wiki/Piper_J-3_Cub "Piper J-3 Cub"), repulsed the attacks, inflicting heavy losses. On the following day, the Iraqis renewed their assaults and were again repulsed, with numerous Iraqi armored cars that managed to break into the fort's courtyard put out of action by molotov cocktails. The Iraqis then laid siege to the kibbutz for five days. Israeli counterattacks against the Iraqis on Camel Hill were unsuccessful. After another Iraqi assault failed, the Iraqis attempted to take a nearby hill to the west dominated by [Belvoir Castle](/wiki/Belvoir_Castle_(Israel) "Belvoir Castle (Israel)") but were beaten back by Israeli troops of the [Golani Brigade](/wiki/Golani_Brigade "Golani Brigade") dug in at the top who received artillery support from two *[Napoleonchik](/wiki/Canon_de_65_M_(montagne)_modele_1906 "Canon de 65 M (montagne) modele 1906")* 65mm cannons that had been decisively used against the Syrians several miles to the north days before.[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28) Following this defeat, Iraqi forces retreated and moved into the strategic triangle in Samaria bounded by the Arab towns [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin") and [Tulkarm](/wiki/Tulkarm "Tulkarm").

Iraqi forces in northern Samaria were largely inactive until Glubb requested that they take action to ease pressure on the Arab Legion at Latrun. On 28 May the Iraqis attacked and took part of moshav [Geulim](/wiki/Geulim "Geulim"), near [Netanya](/wiki/Netanya "Netanya"). Israeli troops of the [Alexandroni Brigade](/wiki/Alexandroni_Brigade "Alexandroni Brigade") counterattacked and drove them out. For the next three days Iraqi forces were periodically attacked by Israeli aircraft. The Iraqis subsequently captured a water pumping station between Geulim and [Lydda](/wiki/Lod "Lod") but otherwise made no further offensive action. The Israelis decided to follow up their successes in repulsing the Iraqi attacks with an offensive against Iraqi forces in Samaria. From 28 to 30 May Golani Brigade units advanced south from the Jezreel Valley and captured numerous Arab villages defended only by local militamen in the [Mount Gilboa](/wiki/Mount_Gilboa "Mount Gilboa") area, paving the way for an assault on Jenin. The IDF subsequently assaulted Jenin on 31 May and heavy fighting ensued. The Iraqi forces and local irregulars managed to hold out until Iraqi reinforcements arrived from [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"). They also received air support. The Israelis retreated on 4 June.[[202]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p60-208)[[203]](#cite_note-morris-248-209) The IDF lost 34 killed and over 100 wounded in the fighting for Jenin and claimed to have killed some 200 Iraqi soldiers and Arab irregulars. Historian Pesach Malovany wrote that the Israeli figure for Arab casualties was "somewhat exaggerated" and cited Iraqi losses as 27 killed, while a count of Iraqi graves in a local cemetery suggests a figure of 44 Iraqi soldiers killed.[[204]](#cite_note-210)[[205]](#cite_note-211)

On 4 June, the IDF launched an attack against [Qaqun](/wiki/Qaqun "Qaqun"), northwest of [Tulkarm](/wiki/Tulkarm "Tulkarm"). Elements of the Alexandroni Brigade conquered the village, engaging local militiamen and Iraqi forces. According Benny Morris, only a few local militiamen and several dozen Iraqi soldiers were present and they were rapidly overwhelmed by the Israeli assault. He characterized it as a minor Israeli success. According to the Alexandroni Brigade's official history, a nearby Iraqi headquarters was taken in addition to the village, the Israeli soldiers subsequently held out against Iraqi counterattacks with both sides receiving air support, and an entire Iraqi battalion was ultimately wiped out. Pesarch Malovany wrote that after the village's conquest an Iraqi counterattack was repulsed on 5 June. The Alexandroni Brigade lost 16 soldiers in the fighting for Qaqun.[[206]](#cite_note-212)[[207]](#cite_note-213)[[208]](#cite_note-malovany-44-214)[[209]](#cite_note-215)[[203]](#cite_note-morris-248-209)

After these battles, the Iraqi forces became stationary.[[143]](#cite_note-Pollack149-155-149)[[202]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p60-208) According to Benny Morris, although the IDF had been defeated in fighting at Jenin, that battle along with the conquest of Qaqun convinced the Iraqis to stay in the triangle region, averting the possibility of an Iraqi thrust across Israel's narrow territory in the region to the Mediterranean.[[203]](#cite_note-morris-248-209)

According to Pesach Malovany, additional combat took place in the [Kafr Qasim](/wiki/Kafr_Qasim "Kafr Qasim") area when an Israeli attack on Iraqi forces in the area forced an Iraqi retreat on the night of 11-12 June, with a subsequent Iraqi counterattack repulsed. Iraqi forces subsequently withdrew from the sector entirely, leading to the IDF taking it over.[[208]](#cite_note-malovany-44-214)

#### Northern front – Sea of Galilee

Further information: [Battles of the Kinarot Valley](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Kinarot_Valley "Battles of the Kinarot Valley")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Deganiatank1.jpg/250px-Deganiatank1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deganiatank1.jpg)

Syrian [R-35](/wiki/Renault_R35 "Renault R35") [light tank](/wiki/Light_tank "Light tank") knocked out at [Degania Alef](/wiki/Degania_Alef "Degania Alef")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Fighter_at_Shaar_Hagolan.jpg/250px-Fighter_at_Shaar_Hagolan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fighter_at_Shaar_Hagolan.jpg)

A defender of kibbutz Sha'ar HaGolan at a position overlooking the border

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Palmachnik_at_Tzemach.jpg/250px-Palmachnik_at_Tzemach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palmachnik_at_Tzemach.jpg)

An Israeli soldier at Samakh

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Degania_after_battle.jpg/250px-Degania_after_battle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Degania_after_battle.jpg)

A building in one of the Deganias damaged by Syrian bombardment

Syrian forces entered Palestine on 15 May. The only Syrian Army formation fully ready for action was the 1st Brigade, which had two infantry battalions, an armored battalion consisting of a company of French-built [R 35](/wiki/Renault_R35 "Renault R35") and R 37 tanks and two armored car companies, and a handful of artillery batteries. The 2nd Brigade was still being organized. It would field two infantry battalions and an armored car battalion.[[210]](#cite_note-pollack2002p448-457-216)[[211]](#cite_note-217) The Israeli forces facing the Syrian invasion initially consisted of the [Golani Brigade](/wiki/Golani_Brigade "Golani Brigade")'s 12th Battalion and militia from local kibbutzim, with a company of the [Yiftach Brigade](/wiki/Yiftach_Brigade "Yiftach Brigade") and militia platoons from settlements further behind the line arriving as reinforcements in the following days.[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28)[[212]](#cite_note-Tal,_2004,_pp._251-218)

In the early hours of 15 May, the Syrian invasion began. The 2nd Brigade advanced to the eastern shore of the [Sea of Galilee](/wiki/Sea_of_Galilee "Sea of Galilee") and attacked kibbutz [Ein Gev](/wiki/Ein_Gev "Ein Gev") as a diversion for the main assault conducted by the 1st Brigade. The attack on Ein Gev began with an airstrike by a lone Syrian aircraft which dropped bombs aimed at the kibbutz which missed. Syrian infantry raked the kibbutz with machine gun fire but did not mount a direct ground assault. The 1st Brigade invaded along the southern end of the Sea of Galilee, shelling kibbutzim to their west, and advanced to the lower [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley "Jordan Valley"). An assault on [Sha'ar HaGolan](/wiki/Sha%27ar_HaGolan "Sha'ar HaGolan") and two assaults on [Samakh](/wiki/Samakh,_Tiberias "Samakh, Tiberias") were repelled.[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28) However, the Syrians succeeded in counquering Samakh with a third attack on 18 May. The defenders were shelled by Syrian artillery as they retreated. The members of Sha'ar HaGolan and [Masada](/wiki/Masada_(kibbutz) "Masada (kibbutz)") also fled to [Afikim](/wiki/Afikim "Afikim") and the abandoned kibbutzim were then looted by local Arabs.[[210]](#cite_note-pollack2002p448-457-216)[[22]](#cite_note-morris2008p236-28)

On the night of 18-19 May, an Israeli counterattack to retake Samakh was repelled. On that same night, two Israeli commando raids were launched. In one raid, a platoon from Ein Gev conducted a seaborne landing to the south at Samra and attacked Syrian forces at Tel al-Qasir. The attack was unsuccessful but may have delayed the subsequent Syrian push to [Degania Alef](/wiki/Degania_Alef "Degania Alef") and [Degania Bet](/wiki/Degania_Bet "Degania Bet"), giving the Israelis more time to prepare. In the second raid, a company crossed the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") and attacked a Syrian camp near the [B'not Ya'akov Bridge](/wiki/Daughters_of_Jacob_Bridge "Daughters of Jacob Bridge") north of the Sea of Galilee. The Syrians retreated after a brief firefight and the Israelis destroyed the camp and a number of vehicles without taking losses.[[213]](#cite_note-morris-255-219)

By 19 May, Israeli morale had plummeted. The Haganah sent reinforcements and chief of operations [Yigael Yadin](/wiki/Yigael_Yadin "Yigael Yadin") ordered that the defenders should fight for every position with no voluntary abandonment. Four *[Napoleonchik](/wiki/Canon_de_65_M_(montagne)_modele_1906 "Canon de 65 M (montagne) modele 1906")* 65mm mountain guns were sent to provide artillery support. Prime Minister Ben-Gurion had initially wanted to send them to the Jerusalem front but after an argument with Yadin backed down and agreed to have them sent to the north. On 20 May, the Syrians assaulted Degania Alef and Degania Bet. The two kibbutzim were defended by local militiamen reinforced by elements of the [Carmeli Brigade](/wiki/Carmeli_Brigade "Carmeli Brigade"). The defenders were supported by three 20mm guns at Beit Yerah, four 81mm mortars of which were positioned at [Kvutzat Kinneret](/wiki/Kvutzat_Kinneret "Kvutzat Kinneret") and one at Degania Alef, and a [Davidka](/wiki/Davidka "Davidka") mortar at Degania Alef. Each kibbutz also had a [PIAT](/wiki/PIAT "PIAT") with fifteen rounds. The defenders were ordered to fight to the death and not retreat.[[213]](#cite_note-morris-255-219)

The Syrians began their attacks with a half-hour artillery barrage on both kibbutzim, followed by an advance on Degania Alef by Syrian infantry and armor setting out from the police fort near Samakh. The attack was decisively repelled. The Syrian infantry were pinned down by Israeli fire while the Syrian armor retreated after numerous tanks and armored cars were hit by 20mm cannon and PIAT fire as well as grenades and molotov cocktails. The Syrians followed up with an attack on Degania Bet, which was also stopped by fierce resistance. However, the Syrians remained dug in beyond the perimeter fences of both kibbutzim and proceeded to harass them with cannon and small arms fire. The stalemate was broken the following day when the four *Napoleonchik* mountain guns arrived and were positioned on the heights of [Alumot](/wiki/Alumot "Alumot"), overlooking the area. The Israelis used them to conduct an artillery barrage against the Syrian headquarters at Samakh and Syrian infantry and armor between Samakh and the two kibbutzim - Israel's first use of artillery in the war. The barrage surprised the Syrians and caused them to retreat to Tel al-Qasir, abandoning Samakh, Masada, and Sha'ar HaGolan along the way.[[214]](#cite_note-220)[[210]](#cite_note-pollack2002p448-457-216)[[202]](#cite_note-Karsh2002p60-208)

The defeat was considered to be decisive. Based on these battles, British observers concluded that the Arabs would not win the war. The Syrian Defense Minister and Chief of Staff as well as the commanders of the 1st and 2nd Brigades resigned within days.[[215]](#cite_note-morris-257-221) One author claims that the main reason for the Syrian defeat was the Syrian soldiers' low regard for the Israelis who they believed would not stand and fight.[[212]](#cite_note-Tal,_2004,_pp._251-218) The Syrians claimed that their defeat was due to unpreparedness, the quality of Israeli fortifications, and a lack of coordination between various Syrian units as well between the Syrian and Iraqi armies.[[215]](#cite_note-morris-257-221)

On 6 June, Syrian forces attacked [Mishmar HaYarden](/wiki/Mishmar_HaYarden_(moshava) "Mishmar HaYarden (moshava)"), but were repulsed. On 10 June, the Syrians overran Mishmar HaYarden in house-to-house fighting and advanced to the main road, where they were stopped by units of the [Oded Brigade](/wiki/Oded_Brigade "Oded Brigade").[[216]](#cite_note-222) Subsequently, the Syrians reverted to a defensive posture, conducting only a few minor attacks on small, exposed Israeli settlements.[[210]](#cite_note-pollack2002p448-457-216)

#### Galilee Panhandle

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008137129105171%29.jpg/250px-1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008137129105171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab-Israeli_War_(997008137129105171).jpg)

Israeli soldiers in Al-Malkiyya

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/PikiWiki_Israel_20772_The_Palmach.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_20772_The_Palmach.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_20772_The_Palmach.jpg)

[Fawzi al-Qawukji](/wiki/Fawzi_al-Qawukji "Fawzi al-Qawukji"), the [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army") commander

The [Galilee Panhandle](/wiki/Galilee_Panhandle "Galilee Panhandle"), which was bordered by Lebanon to the west and north and Syria to the east, was to be invaded by the [Lebanese Army](/wiki/Lebanese_Armed_Forces "Lebanese Armed Forces") according to the Arab League invasion plan, which called for a Lebanese push to [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel") and [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"). The Lebanese Army at the time consisted of four infantry battalions, two artillery batteries, and an armored battalion equipped with light tanks and armored cars. However, the planned invasion was called off at shortly before it was to go ahead largely due to domestic political consideration, as well as the recognition that the Lebanese Army was ill-prepared for war and that entering the war could result in an Israeli conquest of southern Lebanon. In addition, the Haganah had also captured the route the Lebanese war plan had designated for use in the invasion in [Operation Ben-Ami](/wiki/Operation_Ben-Ami "Operation Ben-Ami") just before the end of the Mandate. However, in order to maintain its reputation in the Arab world, Lebanon agreed to facilitate an invasion by the [Arab Liberation Army](/wiki/Arab_Liberation_Army "Arab Liberation Army") and provide it with artillery cover, armored cars, personnel, and logistical support. The ALA force that entered Palestine from Lebanon was the 2nd Yarmuk Battalion, which consisted of several hundred Iraqi, Syrian, Lebanese, and Yugoslav volunteers. Israel had the [Yiftach Brigade](/wiki/Yiftach_Brigade "Yiftach Brigade") stationed in the area to defend it.[[217]](#cite_note-morris-258-223)

On 15 May, the 2nd Yarmuk Battalion crossed the border and pushed to the abandoned village of [Al-Malkiyya](/wiki/Al-Malkiyya "Al-Malkiyya") and the surrounding area. The area was defended by the Yiftach Brigade's 1st Battalion and fighting commenced in and around the village. The ALA fighters received artillery support and were reinforced by a company of Jordanian Bedouin volunteers, and the Haganah ultimately ended up retreating by nightfall. The Israelis had taken around 150 casualties. Although victorious, the ALA had also taken serious casualties and decided to dig in at Al-Malkiyya and the nearby village of [Qadas](/wiki/Kedesh "Kedesh") rather than advance further. On the night of 15-16 May, a bridge over the [Litani River](/wiki/Litani_River "Litani River") six miles into Lebanon was destroyed in an Israeli commando raid, which reduced Lebanon's ability to supply the ALA forces. On the night of 28-29 May, the Yiftach Brigade counterattacked and retook Al-Malkiyya and the cost of 2 killed and 3 wounded. On 5-6 June, the 3rd Battalion of the Lebanese Army, together with ALA fighters and a Syrian battalion, recaptured Al-Malkiyya and Qadas in what became the only intervention of the Lebanese Army during the war, handing the towns over to the ALA and withdrawing on 8 July.[[12]](#cite_note-Hughes-16)[[186]](#cite_note-morris-260-192)

#### Haifa area

On the night 22-23 May, the 33rd Battalion of the Haganah's Alexandroni Brigade attacked the Palestinian Arab village of [Tantura](/wiki/Tantura "Tantura"), which was part of an Arab enclave south of Haifa. The attack began with heavy machine gun fire followed by an infantry assault from all landward sides as an Israeli naval vessel blocked off the village from the sea. The villagers put up fierce resistance but Tantura was conquered on 23 May.[[218]](#cite_note-224) Subsequently, Israeli troops killed a number of villagers in what became known as the [Tantura massacre](/wiki/Tantura_massacre "Tantura massacre"), although the number of those killed is disputed.

#### Air operations

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Egyptian_Plane_TA_1948.jpg/250px-Egyptian_Plane_TA_1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_Plane_TA_1948.jpg)

An Egyptian [Spitfire](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire") shot down over Tel Aviv on 15 May 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136108005171%29.jpg/250px-1948_Arab-Israeli_War_%28997008136108005171%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:1948_Arab-Israeli_War_(997008136108005171).jpg)

The Egyptian bombing of Tel Aviv on 15 May 1948

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Egyptian_bombing_1948.jpg/250px-Egyptian_bombing_1948.jpg)](/wiki/File:Egyptian_bombing_1948.jpg)

Volunteers evacuating a wounded man in the aftermath of an Egyptian attack on Tel Aviv

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/114340_tel_aviv_PikiWiki_Israel.jpg/250px-114340_tel_aviv_PikiWiki_Israel.jpg)](/wiki/File:114340_tel_aviv_PikiWiki_Israel.jpg)

Buses damaged in the Egyptian attack on the Tel Aviv central bus station

The Haganah's embryonic air corps, *[Sherut Avir](/wiki/Sherut_Avir "Sherut Avir")* ("Air Service"), was established in November 1947 and became the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") following the establishment of the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces"). At the beginning of the war, Israel had no combat aircraft, only a hodge-podge of civilian aircraft which were used as makeshift bombers by having aircrew carry 25 and 50 pound bombs and incendiaries in their lap and manually drop them.[[219]](#cite_note-machal-225) Of the invading Arab nations, Egypt, Syria, and Iraq had air forces with fighter and bomber aircraft. However, many of their aircraft were unserviceable and they suffered from low competence among pilots as well as poor maintenance, ground control, and intelligence. Losses and diminishing ammunition stocks over the course of the war further reduced their effectiveness. Meanwhile, Israel gradually acquired combat aircraft, first fielding them in late May, and its air force only grew in strength.[[220]](#cite_note-Morris_2008,_p._261-226)

On 15 May, with the beginning of the war, four [Royal Egyptian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Egyptian_Air_Force "Royal Egyptian Air Force") (REAF) Spitfires attacked [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), bombing [Sde Dov Airfield](/wiki/Sde_Dov_Airport "Sde Dov Airport"), where the bulk of Sherut Avir's aircraft were concentrated, as well as the [Reading Power Station](/wiki/Reading_Power_Station "Reading Power Station"). Several aircraft were destroyed, some others were damaged, and five people were killed. Throughout the following hours, additional waves of Egyptian aircraft bombed and strafed targets around Tel Aviv, although these raids had little effect. One Spitfire was shot down by machine gun fire and its pilot was taken prisoner.[[220]](#cite_note-Morris_2008,_p._261-226)[[219]](#cite_note-machal-225)

Throughout following days, the REAF continued to attack Tel Aviv, causing civilian casualties. On 18 May, Egyptian warplanes [attacked](/wiki/Egyptian_airstrike_on_the_Old_Tel_Aviv_central_bus_station "Egyptian airstrike on the Old Tel Aviv central bus station") the [Tel Aviv central bus station](/wiki/Old_Tel_Aviv_central_bus_station "Old Tel Aviv central bus station"), killing 42 people and wounding over 100. In addition to their attacks on Tel Aviv, the Egyptians bombed Israeli ground troops, towns, rural settlements and airfields, though few casualties were caused by these raids.[[220]](#cite_note-Morris_2008,_p._261-226) At the outset of the war, the REAF was able to attack Israel with near impunity, due to the lack of Israeli fighter aircraft to intercept them.[[221]](#cite_note-227)

On 22 May, Egyptian Spitfires attacked [RAF Ramat David](/wiki/Ramat_David_Airbase "Ramat David Airbase"), southeast of Haifa, while the base was still held by the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") as it covered the withdrawal of British forces from Palestine. The Egyptians mistakenly believed that the Israelis had already taken over the base. The attack destroyed and damaged numerous aircraft, destroyed a hangar, and killed four RAF personnel. Five attacking Egyptian Spitfires were shot down by the RAF.[[222]](#cite_note-228)[[223]](#cite_note-229) The REAF took additional losses as the Israelis fielded more effective anti-aircraft defenses. By the end of May, the Egyptians had lost almost the entire Spitfire squadron based at [El Arish](/wiki/El_Arish "El Arish"), including many of their best pilots.[[171]](#cite_note-morris-235-177)

In the first weeks of the war, Israeli light aircraft bombed Arab encampments and columns. The raids were mostly carried out at night to avoid interception by Arab fighter aircraft. These attacks usually had little effect, except on morale.[[220]](#cite_note-Morris_2008,_p._261-226)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Avia-S-199-IAF-101Sqn-Tel-Nof-Israel-1948-01.jpg/250px-Avia-S-199-IAF-101Sqn-Tel-Nof-Israel-1948-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avia-S-199-IAF-101Sqn-Tel-Nof-Israel-1948-01.jpg)

An Avia S-199, Israel's 1st fighter aircraft

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Spitfire-MkIX-hatzerim-1-2.jpg/250px-Spitfire-MkIX-hatzerim-1-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spitfire-MkIX-hatzerim-1-2.jpg)

An Israeli Spitfire F Mk

The balance of air power soon began to swing in favor of the Israeli Air Force following the arrival of 25 [Avia S-199s](/wiki/Avia_S-199 "Avia S-199") from [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), the first of which arrived in Israel on 20 May. Ironically, Israel was using the Avia S-199, an inferior derivative of the [Bf 109](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Bf_109 "Messerschmitt Bf 109") designed in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") to counter British-designed Spitfires flown by Egypt. Throughout the rest of the war, Israel would acquire more Avia fighters, as well as 62 Spitfires from Czechoslovakia. On 28 May 1948, Sherut Avir became the Israeli Air Force.[[224]](#cite_note-230)

On 3 June, Israel scored its first victory in aerial combat when Israeli pilot [Modi Alon](/wiki/Modi_Alon "Modi Alon") shot down a pair of Egyptian DC-3s that had just bombed Tel Aviv. Although Tel Aviv would see additional raids by fighter aircraft, there would be no more raids by bombers for the rest of the war. From then on, the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") began engaging the Arab air forces in air-to-air combat. The first dogfight took place on 8 June, when an Israeli fighter plane flown by Gideon Lichtman shot down an Egyptian Spitfire. By the fall of 1948, the IAF had achieved [air superiority](/wiki/Air_superiority "Air superiority") and had superior firepower and more knowledgeable personnel, many of whom had seen action in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[225]](#cite_note-231) Many of the pilots who fought for the Israeli Air Force were [foreign volunteers](/wiki/Mahal_(Israel) "Mahal (Israel)") or mercenaries, including many World War II veterans.[[226]](#cite_note-232)

Following Israeli air attacks on Egyptian and Iraqi columns, the Egyptians repeatedly bombed [Ekron Airfield](/wiki/Tel_Nof_Airbase "Tel Nof Airbase"), where IAF fighters were based. During a 30 May raid, bombs aimed for Ekron hit central [Rehovot](/wiki/Rehovot "Rehovot"), killing 7 civilians and wounding 30. In response to this, and probably to the Jordanian victories in the [Battles of Latrun](/wiki/Battles_of_Latrun_(1948) "Battles of Latrun (1948)"), Israel began bombing targets in Arab cities. On the night of 31 May/1 June, the first Israeli raid on an Arab capital took place when three Israeli aircraft attacked [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), dropping several dozen 55 and 110-pound bombs, hitting the King's Palace and an adjacent British airfield. Some 12 people were killed and 30 wounded. During the attack, an RAF hangar was damaged, as were some British aircraft. The British threatened that in the event of another such attack, they would shoot down the attacking aircraft and bomb Israeli airfields, and as a result, Israeli aircraft did not attack Amman again for the rest of the war. On 11 June, hours before the first truce came into effect, the Israeli Air Force carried out a raid on [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") with a lone [C-47 Skytrain](/wiki/Douglas_C-47_Skytrain "Douglas C-47 Skytrain") dropping explosive and incendiary bombs over the city, killing 22 people.[[227]](#cite_note-morris-262-233)[[228]](#cite_note-234)

#### Sea battles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Northland_Color_1.jpg/250px-Northland_Color_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northland_Color_1.jpg)

*Northland* in [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") *circa* 1944 which became the INS Eilat

At the outset of the war, the [Israeli Navy](/wiki/Israeli_Navy "Israeli Navy") consisted of three former [Aliyah Bet](/wiki/Aliyah_Bet "Aliyah Bet") ships that had been seized by the British and impounded in [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") harbour, where they were tied up at the breakwater. Work on establishing a navy had begun shortly before Israeli independence, and the three ships were selected due to them having a military background – one, the INS *Eilat*, was the ex-[US Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") gunboat [USCGC *Northland*](/wiki/USCGC_Northland_(WPG-49) "USCGC Northland (WPG-49)"), and the other two, the INS *Haganah* and INS *Wedgwood*, had been [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") corvettes.[[229]](#cite_note-navy-235)[[230]](#cite_note-Gershoni,_pp._46–47-236) Later, the former [US Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") [submarine chaser](/wiki/Submarine_chaser "Submarine chaser") USS PC-1265 was purchased from the United States and became the INS *Noga*. It arrived in Israel in September 1948.[[231]](#cite_note-237)

The ships were put into minimum running condition by contractors dressed as stevedores and port personnel, who were able to work in the engine rooms and below deck. The work had to be clandestine to avoid arousing British suspicion. On 21 May 1948, the three ships set sail for Tel Aviv, and were made to look like ships that had been purchased by foreign owners for commercial use. In Tel Aviv, the ships were fitted with small field guns dating to the late 19th century and anti-aircraft guns.[[229]](#cite_note-navy-235)[[230]](#cite_note-Gershoni,_pp._46–47-236)

The only significant naval combat until the first truce took place on 2-4 June. An Egyptian corvette shelled [Caesarea](/wiki/Caesarea_(modern_town) "Caesarea (modern town)"), which was home to a small Israeli naval station, and withdrew, causing little damage and no casualties. On 4 June, a flotilla of three Egyptian warships consisting of a corvette, landing craft, and armed troop carrier, appeared off Tel Aviv, possibly intending to shell the city or launch a commando raid. They were engaged by the INS *Eilat*, which was kept at bay by the larger guns of the Egyptian ships. Three Israeli aircraft then attacked the Egyptian ships, dropping bombs and strafing them. One of the Egyptian ships was hit by a bomb and one of the Israeli aircraft was shot down. The Egyptian warships then withdrew under cover of a smokescreen.[[227]](#cite_note-morris-262-233)

#### Israeli use of biological warfare

Main article: [Operation Cast Thy Bread](/wiki/Operation_Cast_Thy_Bread "Operation Cast Thy Bread")

Research by Israeli historians [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") and [Benjamin Kedar](/wiki/Benjamin_Kedar "Benjamin Kedar") show that during the 1948 war, Israel conducted a [biological warfare operation codenamed Cast Thy Bread](/wiki/Operation_Cast_Thy_Bread "Operation Cast Thy Bread"). According to Morris and Kedar, the Haganah initially used [typhoid](/wiki/Typhoid_fever "Typhoid fever") bacteria to contaminate water wells in newly cleared Arab villages to prevent the population including militiamen from returning. Later, the biological warfare campaign expanded to include Jewish settlements that were in imminent danger of being captured by Arab troops and inhabited Arab towns not slated for capture. There were also plans to expand the biological warfare campaign into other Arab states including Egypt, Lebanon and Syria, but they were not carried out.[[232]](#cite_note-238)

#### End of the first phase

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Palestine_Military_Situation%2C_June_11%2C_1948%2C_Truman_Papers.jpg/250px-Palestine_Military_Situation%2C_June_11%2C_1948%2C_Truman_Papers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palestine_Military_Situation,_June_11,_1948,_Truman_Papers.jpg)

The Palestine Military Situation, 11 June 1948. Truman Papers

The first four weeks of the war were a decisive Israeli success. The Israelis managed to hold off the Arab forces, managing to keep most of their territory and expand their holdings. According to [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris"), in retrospect this had been the only period of the war in which the Arabs could have won or at least made major territorial gains Israeli expense. The Syrians were stopped just west of the original border between Palestine and Syria, the Jordanians and Iraqis occupied territory that had been allotted to the Palestinian Arabs, with the Jewish Quarter of the Old City of Jerusalem being the only Israeli-held territory that they managed to capture, and the Egyptians were stopped roughly at the northern limit of the southern part of Palestine that had been intended to go to the Arabs, although they did manage to cut off some Israeli troops and settlements. The Israelis expanded their territorial holdings in some areas, such as the Western Galilee and Jerusalem area. The IDF was also larger and better equipped than at the beginning of the war. The Israelis had also moved onto the offensive, and while initial counterattacks at Latrun, Isdud, and Jenin failed, the strategic initiative passed into their hands.[[233]](#cite_note-239)

Upon the implementation of the truce, the IDF had control over nine Arab cities and towns or mixed cities and towns: New [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa"), [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"), [Acre](/wiki/Acre,_Israel "Acre, Israel"), [Safed](/wiki/Safed "Safed"), [Tiberias](/wiki/Tiberias "Tiberias"), [Baysan](/wiki/Baysan "Baysan") (Beit She'an), [Samakh](/wiki/Samakh,_Tiberias "Samakh, Tiberias") and [Yibna](/wiki/Yibna "Yibna") ([Yavne](/wiki/Yavne "Yavne")). Another city, [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), was not occupied but its residents fled. The combined Arab forces captured 14 Jewish settlement points, but only one of them, [Mishmar HaYarden](/wiki/Mishmar_HaYarden "Mishmar HaYarden"), was in the territory of the proposed Jewish State according to [Resolution 181](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine").[[234]](#cite_note-statsgelber-240)

Within the boundaries of the proposed Jewish state, there were twelve Arab villages which opposed Jewish control or were captured by the invading Arab armies, and in addition to them, the [Lod Airport](/wiki/Lod_Airport "Lod Airport") and pumping station near [Antipatris](/wiki/Antipatris "Antipatris"), which were within the boundaries of the proposed Jewish state, were under the control of the Arabs. The IDF captured about 50 large Arab villages outside of the boundaries of the proposed Jewish State and a larger number of hamlets and Bedouin encampments. 350 square kilometres of the proposed Jewish State were under the control of the Arab forces, while 700 square kilometres of the proposed Arab State were under the control of the IDF. This figure ignores the Negev desert which was not under any absolute control of either side.[[234]](#cite_note-statsgelber-240)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
