During the [1947–1948 Civil War in Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/1947%E2%80%931948_Civil_War_in_Mandatory_Palestine "1947–1948 Civil War in Mandatory Palestine") and the 1948 Arab–Israeli War that followed, around 750,000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled from their homes, out of approximately 1,200,000 Arabs living in former [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"), a displacement known to Palestinians as the *[Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba")*. In 1951, the UN Conciliation Commission for Palestine estimated that the number of Palestinian refugees displaced from Israel was 711,000.[[294]](#cite_note-300)

This number did not include [displaced Palestinians inside Israeli-held territory](/wiki/Present_absentee "Present absentee"). More than 400 Arab villages, and about ten Jewish villages and neighbourhoods, [were depopulated](/wiki/List_of_villages_depopulated_during_the_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "List of villages depopulated during the Arab–Israeli conflict") during the Arab–Israeli conflict, most of them during 1948. According to estimate based on earlier census, the total Muslim population in Palestine was 1,143,336 in 1947.[[295]](#cite_note-301) The [causes of the 1948 Palestinian exodus](/wiki/Causes_of_the_1948_Palestinian_exodus "Causes of the 1948 Palestinian exodus") are a controversial topic among historians.[[296]](#cite_note-302) After the war, around 156,000 [Arabs remained](/wiki/Arab_citizens_of_Israel "Arab citizens of Israel") in Israel and became Israeli citizens.[[297]](#cite_note-303)

Displaced Palestinian Arabs, known as [Palestinian refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugee "Palestinian refugee"), were settled in [Palestinian refugee camps](/wiki/Palestine_refugee_camps "Palestine refugee camps") throughout the Arab world. The United Nations established [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA") as a relief and human development agency tasked with providing humanitarian assistance to Palestinian refugees. Arab nations refused to absorb Palestinian refugees, instead keeping them in refugee camps while insisting that they be allowed to return.[[298]](#cite_note-Palestine_refugees-UNRWA-304)[[299]](#cite_note-Jerusalem_Fund-305)

Refugee status was also passed on to their descendants, who were also largely denied citizenship in Arab states, except in Transjordan.[[300]](#cite_note-306) The [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League") instructed its members to deny Palestinians citizenship "to avoid dissolution of their identity and protect their right of return to their homeland." More than 1.4 million Palestinians still live in 58 recognised refugee camps,[[298]](#cite_note-Palestine_refugees-UNRWA-304)[[299]](#cite_note-Jerusalem_Fund-305) while more than 5 million [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians#Demographics "Palestinians") live outside Israel and the [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories").

Palestinian refugees and displaced persons and the lack of a [Palestinian right of return](/wiki/Palestinian_right_of_return "Palestinian right of return") remain major issues in the [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict").

#### Jews

Main article: [Jewish exodus from Arab countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab countries")

In the three years from May 1948 to the end of 1951, 700,000 Jews [settled in Israel](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah"), mainly along the borders and in former Arab lands,[[17]](#cite_note-BennyMorris2-22) doubling the Jewish population there.[[301]](#cite_note-307) Of these, upwards of 300,000 [arrived from Asian and North African states](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries").[[302]](#cite_note-sachar_pp395-403-308)[[303]](#cite_note-309) Among them, the largest group, over 100,000, [was from Iraq](/wiki/Operation_Ezra_and_Nehemiah "Operation Ezra and Nehemiah"). The remaining came mostly from Europe, including 136,000 from the 250,000 displaced Jews of World War II living in [refugee camps](/wiki/Displaced_persons_camps_in_post-World_War_II_Europe "Displaced persons camps in post-World War II Europe") and urban centers in Germany, Austria, and Italy,[[304]](#cite_note-310) and more than 270,000 coming from Eastern Europe,[[305]](#cite_note-311) mainly [Romania](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Romania#Post-War "History of the Jews in Romania") and [Poland](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Poland#Communist_rule:_1945–1989 "History of the Jews in Poland"), over 100,000 each.

On the establishment of the state, a top priority was given to a policy for the "ingathering of exiles", and the [Mossad LeAliyah Bet](/wiki/Mossad_LeAliyah_Bet "Mossad LeAliyah Bet") gave key assistance to the Jewish Agency to organise immigrants from Europe and the Middle East, and arrange for their transport to Israel. For Ben-Gurion, a fundamental defect of the State was that "it lacked Jews".[[306]](#cite_note-312)

Jewish immigrants from Arab and Muslim countries left for numerous reasons. The war's outcome had exacerbated Arab hostilities to local Jewish communities. News of the victory aroused messianic expectations in Libya and Yemen; Zionism had taken root in many countries; active incentives for making aliyah formed a key part of Israeli policy; and better economic prospects and security were to be expected from a Jewish state.[[302]](#cite_note-sachar_pp395-403-308)

Some Arab governments, Egypt, for example, held their Jewish communities hostage at times. Persecution, political instability, and news of a number of violent pogroms also played a role. Some 800,000–1,000,000 Jews eventually left the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") over the next three decades as a result of these various factors.[[302]](#cite_note-sachar_pp395-403-308) An estimated 650,000 of the departees settled in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeker20054-313)

## Historiography

Since the war, different [historiographical](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") traditions have interpreted the events of 1948 differently; in the words of the [New Historian](/wiki/New_Historians "New Historians") [Avi Shlaim](/wiki/Avi_Shlaim "Avi Shlaim"), "each side subscribes to a different version of events."[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) In the Israeli narrative, the war is Israel's War of Independence.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) In the Palestinian narrative, the War of 1948 is inextricable from the [Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba"), the Zionist movement is one of [settler colonialism](/wiki/Zionism_as_settler_colonialism "Zionism as settler colonialism"),[[309]](#cite_note-315) and the Israelis are seen as [conquerors](/wiki/Conquest "Conquest") and the Palestinians as victims.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) The different narratives of 1948 reflect these different perceptions.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

An issue affecting the historiography of 1948 is access to sources and archives, which may have been destroyed, appropriated, censored, or otherwise made unavailable to some or all researchers.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316) Linguistic barriers represent another hurdle, as most research is published exclusively in the author's native language and is not translated.[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316)

The historiography of 1948 is tied to political [legitimacy](/wiki/Legitimacy_(political) "Legitimacy (political)") in the present and has implications for the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").[[311]](#cite_note-Sela-2016-317) According to [Avraham Sela](/wiki/Avraham_Sela "Avraham Sela") and Neil Caplan:

> A major reason for this grip of the past over the present is the unfulfilled quest of both Israelis and Palestinians for legitimacy, in one or more of the following three senses: (a) each party's sense of its own legitimacy as a national community entitled to its own sovereign state; (b) each party's willingness to grant legitimacy to at least part of the competing national narrative of the other; and (c) the international community's extension of legitimacy to the competing rights and claims of Israelis and Palestinians.[[311]](#cite_note-Sela-2016-317)

The narratives of 1948 have also had implications for [Palestinian refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees").[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 211

### Israeli narratives

The Israelis, whether or not they were conquerors, were irrefutably the victors of the war, and for this reason among others, "they were able to propagate more effectively than their opponents their version of this fateful war."[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) Only in 1987 was that narrative effectively challenged outside the Arab world.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

#### Zionist narrative

Avi Shlaim gives the conventional Zionist narrative or the "old history" of the 1948 war as follows:

> The conflict between Jews and Arabs in Palestine came to a head following the passage, on 29 November 1947, of the United Nations partition resolution that called for the establishment of two states, one Jewish and one Arab. The Jews accepted the U.N. plan despite the painful sacrifices it entailed, but the Palestinians, the neighboring Arab states, and the Arab League rejected it. Great Britain did everything in its power toward the end of the Palestine Mandate to frustrate the establishment of the Jewish state envisaged in the UN plan. With the expiry of the Mandate and the proclamation of the State of Israel, seven Arab states sent their armies into Palestine with the firm intention of strangling the Jewish state at birth. The subsequent struggle was an unequal one between a Jewish David and an Arab Goliath. The infant Jewish state fought a desperate, heroic, and ultimately successful battle for survival against overwhelming odds. During the war, hundreds of thousands of Palestinians fled to the neighboring Arab states, mainly in response to orders from their leaders and despite Jewish pleas to stay and demonstrate that peaceful coexistence was possible. After the war, the story continues, Israeli leaders sought peace with all their heart and all their might but there was no one to talk to on the other side. Arab intransigence was alone responsible for the political deadlock, which was not broken until President Anwar Sadat's visit to Jerusalem thirty years later.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

According to Shlaim, this narrative is "not history in the proper sense of the word," as most of the literature on the war was produced – not by professional academic historians – but rather by participants in the war, politicians, soldiers, and state-sponsored historians, as well as by sympathetic journalists, chroniclers, and biographers.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) It also portrays Israelis as morally superior, lacks political analysis, and gives undue weight to "the heroic feats of the Israeli fighters."[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) This [nationalist narrative](/wiki/Nationalist_historiography "Nationalist historiography") was taught in Israeli schools and used for gaining legitimacy internationally.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

#### New Historians

Further information: [New Historians](/wiki/New_Historians "New Historians")

The standard Zionist narrative of the war remained unchallenged outside the Arab world until the war's fortieth anniversary, when a number of critical books came out, including [Simha Flapan](/wiki/Simha_Flapan "Simha Flapan")'s *The Birth of Israel: Myths and Realities* (1987), [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris")'s *The Birth of the Palestinian Refugee Problem* (1987), [Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé")'s *Britain and the Arab-Israeli Conflict, 1948–51* (1988), and Shlaim's *Collusion Across the Jordan: King Abdullah, the Zionist Movement and the Partition of Palestine* (1988).[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) These writers came to be known as [New Historians](/wiki/New_Historians "New Historians") or "post-Zionists".*[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318)*

According to Shlaim, the new historians disagreed with the Zionist narrative on six main points: British policy with regard to the Yishuv at the end of the Palestine Mandate, the military balance in 1948, the origins of the Palestinian refugee problem, the nature of relations between Israelis and Jordanians during the war, Arab aims in the war, and the reasons peace remained elusive after the war.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

Among their most vitriolic critics was [Shabtai Teveth](/wiki/Shabtai_Teveth "Shabtai Teveth"), biographer of [David Ben-Gurion](/wiki/David_Ben-Gurion "David Ben-Gurion"), who published "The New Historians", a series of four weekly full-page articles attacking the new historians, in [Haaretz](/wiki/Haaretz "Haaretz") May 1989.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314) Teveth claimed that the new historiography was flawed in its practice and that it was politically motived, that it was pro-Palestinian and aimed to [delegitimize](/wiki/Delegitimisation_of_Israel "Delegitimisation of Israel") the State of Israel.[[308]](#cite_note-Shlaim-1995-314)

#### Neo-Zionist narratives

[Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé") identifies a turn in predominant Israeli narratives about 1948 in September 2000. In the climate of the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada") and in the [Post-9/11](/wiki/Post-9/11 "Post-9/11") period, "not only were Israel's brutal military operations against the Palestinians during the new intifada seen as justified, but so was their systematic expulsion in 1948."[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318) Evidence of the expulsions, massacres, and war crimes of 1948 brought to light by the New Historians could no longer be ignored, but writers of what Pappé calls a "neo-Zionist" narrative justified these as necessary or unavoidable.[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318)

In this period, the focus of Israeli historical writing on 1948 shifted largely from its human impact back to its military aspects.[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318) Neo-Zionist writers were given selective access to top-secret material, to which writers critical of Zionism would not have been given access, and much of their work was published by the [Israeli Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Israeli_Ministry_of_Defense "Israeli Ministry of Defense").[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318)

Among those Pappé associated with the neo-Zionist perspective were [Benny Morris](/wiki/Benny_Morris "Benny Morris") (who had become more outspokenly defensive of Zionism by this time), [Daniel Gutwein](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_Gutwein&action=edit&redlink=1 "Daniel Gutwein (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%93%D7%A0%D7%99%D7%90%D7%9C_%D7%92%D7%95%D7%98%D7%95%D7%95%D7%99%D7%99%D7%9F "he:דניאל גוטוויין")], [Mordechai Bar-On](/wiki/Mordechai_Bar-On "Mordechai Bar-On"), [Yoav Gelber](/wiki/Yoav_Gelber "Yoav Gelber"), Tamir Goren, [Arnon Golan](/w/index.php?title=Arnon_Golan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arnon Golan (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%90%D7%A8%D7%A0%D7%95%D7%9F_%D7%92%D7%95%D7%9C%D7%9F "he:ארנון גולן")], Alon Kadish, and [Yoav Peleg](/w/index.php?title=Yoav_Peleg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yoav Peleg (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%99%D7%95%D7%90%D7%91_%D7%A4%D7%9C%D7%92 "he:יואב פלג")], as well as the journal [*Techelet*](/wiki/Azure_(magazine) "Azure (magazine)") .[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318)

### Palestinian narratives

Unlike Israeli narratives that shifted over the decades, Palestinian narratives of 1948 have been more or less constant, focusing on Palestinians' indigenous rights to Palestine, Palestinian victimhood, dispossession, displacement, exile, statelessness, and more "unrequited grievances against colonialism and Zionism."[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 209–211 The term '[Nakba](/wiki/Nakba "Nakba")' to describe the Palestinian catastrophe in the war of 1948 was coined in [Constantin Zureiq's](/wiki/Constantin_Zureiq "Constantin Zureiq") 1948 book *[Ma'na an-Nakba](/wiki/Ma%27na_an-Nakba "Ma'na an-Nakba")*.[[313]](#cite_note-319) [Aref al-Aref](/wiki/Aref_al-Aref "Aref al-Aref") wrote a six volume work titled *[an-Nakba](/w/index.php?title=An-Nakba_(book)&action=edit&redlink=1 "An-Nakba (book) (page does not exist)")* [[ar](https://ar.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%86%D9%83%D8%A8%D8%A9_(%D9%83%D8%AA%D8%A7%D8%A8) "ar:النكبة (كتاب)")] that was published in Arabic in the 1950s.[[314]](#cite_note-320)

Palestinian narratives have focused on countering the dominant Zionist narrative; the preeminent Palestinian historian of 1948 [Walid Khalidi](/wiki/Walid_Khalidi "Walid Khalidi") has dedicated much of his career to disproving the official Israeli narrative that the [1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight](/wiki/1948_Palestinian_expulsion_and_flight "1948 Palestinian expulsion and flight") was voluntary.[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 211

[Rashid Khalidi](/wiki/Rashid_Khalidi "Rashid Khalidi") and other historians hold that "there is no established, authoritative Palestinian master narrative."[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 212 They attribute this to, among other reasons, the dispersed and fragmented state of the Palestinian community and the loss, destruction, or appropriation by Israel of relevant documents and libraries.[[315]](#cite_note-321)[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 212 Without access to much in the way of archival materials, Palestinian historians have made use of [oral history](/wiki/Oral_history "Oral history").[[316]](#cite_note-322)[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 214

### Arab narratives

In the narratives of the wider Arab-Muslim world, 1948 is seen as an "Arab debacle", representative of the region's social and political decline from its "glorious distant past".[[310]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-2016-316): 210 The official narratives of Arab states on 1948 tended to be [apologetic](/wiki/Apologia "Apologia") with the goal of defending their political legitimacy, while the [Arab nationalists](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism") wrote with a focus on distilling and extracting historical lessons to galvanize Arab society, politics, and ideology in preparation for the next conflict with Israel – neither approach bridled itself too much with historical accuracy.[[317]](#cite_note-323): 6

### Western narratives

#### In the United States

The American journalist [Joan Peters'](/wiki/Joan_Peters "Joan Peters") 1984 book *[From Time Immemorial](/wiki/From_Time_Immemorial "From Time Immemorial")* had a massive impact on how 1948 was understood in popular and political narratives in the United States.[[318]](#cite_note-324)[[319]](#cite_note-325)

[Ilan Pappé](/wiki/Ilan_Papp%C3%A9 "Ilan Pappé") asserts the neo-Zionist narrative was pushed in the United States most passionately by [Michael Walzer](/wiki/Michael_Walzer "Michael Walzer"), and by [Anita Shapira](/wiki/Anita_Shapira "Anita Shapira") and [Derek Penslar](/wiki/Derek_Penslar "Derek Penslar") with their 2003 *Israeli Historical Revisionism: From Left to Right*.[[312]](#cite_note-Pappe-2009-318)

## In popular culture

* In 1948, the Egyptian film *[A Girl from Palestine](/wiki/A_Girl_from_Palestine "A Girl from Palestine")* tells the story of an Egyptian fighter pilot.[[320]](#cite_note-326)
* A 2015 PBS documentary, *[A Wing and a Prayer](/wiki/A_Wing_and_a_Prayer_(film) "A Wing and a Prayer (film)")*, depicts the Al Schwimmer–led airborne smuggling missions to arm Israel.[[321]](#cite_note-327)

[ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-300-18273-6](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-300-18273-6 "Special:BookSources/978-0-300-18273-6"). "(Ben Gurion) He also did not flinch from provoking the United Nations by breaking the truce agreement. But the limit of his fearlessness was a clash with a Western power. Vainly, the right and Mapam accused him of defeatism. He did not flinch from confronting them but chose to maintain good relations with the United States, which he perceived as a potential ally of the new state, and also not to provoke the British lion, even though its fangs had been drawn. At the end of the war, when Yigal Allon, who represented the younger generation of commanders that had grown up in the war, demanded the conquest of the West Bank up to the Jordan River as the natural, defensible border of the state, Ben-Gurion refused. He recognized that the IDF was militarily strong enough to carry out the conquest, but he believed that the young state should not bite off more than it had already chewed. There was a limit to what the world was prepared to accept. Furthermore, the armistice borders – which later became known as the Green Line – were better than those he had dreamed of at the beginning of the war. In Ben-Gurion's opinion, in terms of territory Israel was satisfied. It was time to send the troops home and start work on building the new nation."
